speaker: Power Development Chairman
prompt: The coal plant near the mangrove forest is built and ready — and so are the protests. "Switch it on and we add a thousand megawatts tomorrow," the Chairman says. "Leave it dark and we have poured a fortune into a monument to indecision." The forest is a UNESCO heritage and the country's storm shield.

left.label: Fire up the coal plant.
left.tooltip: The grid cannot run on heritage.
left.reveal: The lights brighten across three divisions. The forest scientists warn the ash and warm water will gnaw at the mangroves for decades.

right.label: Mothball it and pivot the site to gas or solar.
right.tooltip: The Sundarbans is a wall against the sea; I will not breach it.
right.reveal: You eat the stranded cost and convert the plan. Environmentalists rejoice; the power-hungry industrial belt grumbles in the dark.

speaker: Power Development Chairman
prompt: With the plant running, fishing communities and forest scientists report acidified water and dying spawning grounds. A foreign court is weighing whether to strip the Sundarbans' heritage status — a global humiliation. Your engineers say new scrubbers could cut the damage at a steep price.

left.label: Fund the costly scrubbers and monitoring.
left.tooltip: If it must run, it must not poison.
left.reveal: The retrofit eases the worst harm and saves the heritage listing. The treasury winces; the fisherfolk cautiously return to the water.

right.label: Dispute the science, keep costs down.
right.tooltip: I will not be lectured by foreign committees.
right.reveal: You stonewall and save the money. The heritage status is downgraded, the fisheries keep declining, and the issue becomes a global black mark.

speaker: Cross-Border Power Negotiator
prompt: A giant Indian conglomerate offers electricity from a dedicated coal plant across the border — power on tap, but at a tariff and on terms that auditors call lopsided in the seller's favour, dollar-indexed and locked for twenty-five years.

left.label: Sign the long import contract.
left.tooltip: Reliable power now beats a fair price later.
left.reveal: The grid steadies and a powerful neighbour is pleased. The dollar-indexed bill balloons as the taka falls, draining reserves for decades.

right.label: Renegotiate or walk away.
right.tooltip: I will not chain the grid to a quarter-century of someone else's prices.
right.reveal: You demand fairer terms. The seller threatens to pull out and the supply gap looms — but you keep the country off a 25-year leash.

speaker: Independent Power Producers' Lobby
prompt: For years the state has paid 'capacity charges' to private rental power plants — billions for electricity never generated, locked in by indemnity laws. The lobby, thick with party donors, wants the contracts renewed. An adviser says ending them could save the treasury a fortune.

left.label: Renew the capacity-charge contracts.
left.tooltip: These owners fund the machine that funds me.
left.reveal: The donors are delighted and the idle plants keep cashing cheques. The public learns it pays a king's ransom for power that never flows.

right.label: Let the contracts lapse, hold a real auction.
right.tooltip: I will not pay for electrons that do not exist.
right.reveal: You end the racket and tender competitively. The donors rage and a short-term supply risk appears; the treasury stops haemorrhaging.

speaker: Petrobangla Exploration Chief
prompt: New offshore blocks sit in waters you won at sea tribunal — but exploring them risks reigniting a maritime spat with a neighbour, and only foreign supermajors have the deep-water rigs. The Chief wants to license fast before the gas crisis bites harder.

left.label: License the supermajors aggressively.
left.tooltip: Domestic gas is worth a diplomatic frown.
left.reveal: Rigs move in and the prospect of homegrown gas lifts spirits. The neighbour protests the blocks near the line, chilling relations.

right.label: Go slow with joint surveys and shared blocks.
right.tooltip: Gas under disputed water is gas in a lawsuit.
right.reveal: You pursue cooperative surveys to keep the peace. The crisis deepens while committees map the seabed, and import bills climb.

speaker: Sitakunda Yard Owner
prompt: The beaching yards that scrap the world's dead ships employ tens of thousands and feed the steel mills — and bury workers under falling plate and toxic ash with grim regularity. International rules would force costly 'green' yards. The owner warns reform means ruin and unemployment.

left.label: Keep the beaching yards as they are.
left.tooltip: Steel and jobs outrank inspectors' ideals.
left.reveal: The mills stay fed and the owners loyal. The death toll and the poisoned coastline keep the ugly headlines coming.

right.label: Mandate green ship-recycling standards.
right.tooltip: A yard that buries a worker a week is a slaughterhouse.
right.reveal: You phase in safer yards with a transition fund. Costs rise and some yards close, but the deaths fall and the beaches begin to heal.

speaker: Public Health Hydrologist
prompt: The tube-wells that ended cholera are slowly poisoning millions with natural arsenic — the largest mass poisoning in history, the hydrologist says, and barely on anyone's agenda. Safe piped water or deep wells would cost more than any photo-op returns.

left.label: Fund a national safe-water programme.
left.tooltip: A nation cannot be poisoned by its own taps.
left.reveal: You commit real money to deep wells and filtration. It is unglamorous, expensive, and will quietly save a generation of livers and lives.

right.label: Distribute cheap filters and move on.
right.tooltip: We have louder crises and thinner budgets.
right.reveal: A token filter scheme lets you claim action for little cost. The cancers and lesions keep spreading where the cameras never go.

speaker: Riverbank Erosion Officer
prompt: Illegal sand dredging feeds the construction boom — and eats riverbanks, swallowing villages and bridges into the current. The dredgers are protected by local strongmen tied to your party. The officer wants a real ban; the strongmen want to keep digging.

left.label: Tolerate the dredging for the building boom.
left.tooltip: Cement needs sand, and the party needs the strongmen.
left.reveal: Construction roars and the local bosses stay loyal. Each monsoon the rivers take more homes, and the displaced have your name to curse.

right.label: Ban illegal mining and break the syndicates.
right.tooltip: A bridge in the river is no one's foundation.
right.reveal: You move against the sand mafias. Construction costs rise and a few party bosses turn hostile; the riverbanks, and the villages on them, hold.

speaker: Renewables Director
prompt: Donors dangle cheap green finance for a solar push, but utility-scale panels need flat land — and the only flat land is fertile farmland in a country that must feed itself. The Director asks where the megawatts should come from.

left.label: Convert farmland to solar parks for the cheap finance.
left.tooltip: Clean power now; we will import the rice.
left.reveal: The grid greens fast on concessional money. Displaced farmers and a thinner harvest trade one dependency for another.

right.label: Push rooftop and floating solar instead.
right.tooltip: We will not pave our rice fields to please donors.
right.reveal: You back distributed and floating arrays — slower, dearer per watt, but no farmer loses their field and the food map stays intact.

speaker: Phulbari Coalfield Director
prompt: A foreign firm wants to open-pit mine the great northern coal seam — vast cheap energy, but it would uproot tens of thousands of farmers and drop the water table across the breadbasket. The villages there once died protesting this very mine.

left.label: Approve the open-pit mine.
left.tooltip: The seam could power the nation for decades.
left.reveal: Coal and investment flow north. Whole villages are cleared, the aquifer sinks, and the old protest reignites with funerals behind it.

right.label: Ban open-pit mining for good.
right.tooltip: I will not unhouse the breadbasket for coal.
right.reveal: You kill the project and honour the dead of the old protests. The energy stays underground and the import bill stays high.

speaker: LNG Terminal Operator
prompt: Domestic gas is running dry and the country now depends on imported LNG — but the spot price has spiked fourfold and the terminal operator wants you to keep buying at any cost to avoid blackouts.

left.label: Keep buying LNG at the spike price.
left.tooltip: Lights and looms cannot wait for cheap gas.
left.reveal: The grid holds through the crunch. The import bill devours dollars the reserves can scarcely spare.

right.label: Ration gas and accept rolling blackouts.
right.tooltip: I will not bankrupt us to keep every light on.
right.reveal: You cut industrial gas and load-shed by schedule. Factories idle and tempers fray, but the dollars are saved.

speaker: Power Grid Chief
prompt: A fifth of all electricity generated is lost or stolen — illegal hooks, complicit meter-readers, unpaid VIP connections. The chief wants prepaid meters and a purge of the linemen who run the racket.

left.label: Roll out prepaid meters and purge the racket.
left.tooltip: Pay for the power you burn.
left.reveal: Losses fall and revenue rises sharply. The linemen's union and the freeloading connections — some politically wired — push back hard.

right.label: Leave the connections and the linemen be.
right.tooltip: Do not pick a fight with every substation.
right.reveal: The pilferage continues and the utilities stay broke. The honest customer subsidises the hook on the next pole.

speaker: Industrial Gas Board
prompt: Industry pays a fraction of the cost of its gas, and the board wants a steep price hike to fund new supply. The factory owners — your donors — warn that a hike now closes mills and kills jobs.

left.label: Raise industrial gas prices to cost.
left.tooltip: Subsidised gas is a fire we cannot feed.
left.reveal: The utilities can finally invest in new supply. The owners howl, some mills close, and your donor base cools.

right.label: Hold the subsidised industrial rate.
right.tooltip: Cheap gas keeps the looms turning.
right.reveal: The factories stay competitive on cheap energy. The gas sector stays starved of the cash to find more of it.

speaker: Hilsa Fishery Officer
prompt: The national fish is overfished to the brink; a strict breeding-season ban would let stocks recover but starve a million fishing families for two months. Enforcement means gunboats against desperate boats.

left.label: Enforce the breeding ban hard.
left.tooltip: No fish tomorrow if we take them all today.
left.reveal: Stocks rebound within seasons and the catch later booms. For two months the fishing villages go hungry and angry.

right.label: Allow fishing with token restrictions.
right.tooltip: I will not starve a million families now.
right.reveal: The boats stay out and the families eat this season. The stock thins further toward a collapse that feeds no one.

speaker: Bhola Gas Survey Lead
prompt: A promising onshore gas field sits under a southern island, but tapping it fast needs a foreign supermajor and a pipeline through fragile wetlands. The survey lead wants speed; the ecologists want caution.

left.label: Fast-track the field with a supermajor.
left.tooltip: Homegrown gas now eases everything.
left.reveal: Domestic gas flows sooner and the import bill eases. The pipeline scars the wetlands and the ecologists raise the alarm.

right.label: Develop it slowly with a domestic firm.
right.tooltip: Our own gas, on our own careful terms.
right.reveal: A national company develops it gently over years. The relief is slower; the wetlands and sovereignty are spared.

speaker: Heavy Minerals Concessionaire
prompt: The black sands of the southern beaches are rich in rare minerals a foreign miner will pay well to extract — at the cost of churning up the tourist coast and the turtle nesting grounds.

left.label: Grant the coastal mining concession.
left.tooltip: Wealth lies in the sand we walk on.
left.reveal: Mineral royalties flow into the treasury. The beaches are gouged, the tourism dwindles, and the turtles vanish.

right.label: Protect the coast and refuse the miner.
right.tooltip: Some sand is worth more left lying.
right.reveal: You keep the beaches whole for tourism and turtles. The mineral fortune stays buried and the miner walks.

speaker: Urea Plant Manager
prompt: The state fertiliser plant needs a big gas allocation to run, gas the power sector also wants for the grid. Feed the urea plant and farmers get cheap fertiliser; feed the grid and the lights stay on.

left.label: Allocate the gas to fertiliser.
left.tooltip: Cheap urea is cheap rice is calm.
left.reveal: Domestic urea keeps farmers and food prices steady. The power sector, short of that gas, load-sheds the cities.

right.label: Allocate the gas to the power grid.
right.tooltip: A dark city is a dangerous city.
right.reveal: The grid stays lit through peak demand. Fertiliser output drops, prices rise, and the villages feel the pinch.

speaker: Irrigation Power Officer
prompt: Farmers pump groundwater for the dry-season rice crop on heavily subsidised electricity, draining both the aquifer and the budget. The officer wants to meter and price the irrigation power.

left.label: Meter and price irrigation power.
left.tooltip: Free power drains the water and the treasury.
left.reveal: Pumping grows efficient and the aquifer's fall slows. Farmers, hit with new bills, send their anger up the line.

right.label: Keep irrigation power free and flowing.
right.tooltip: The dry-season crop must not fail.
right.reveal: The harvest is secure and the farmers content. The water table keeps dropping and the subsidy keeps bleeding.

speaker: Waste-to-Energy Promoter
prompt: A foreign firm offers to burn the capital's mountains of garbage for electricity — tidy and modern, but the incinerators emit toxins and undercut the tens of thousands who survive by sorting and recycling waste.

left.label: Build the waste-to-energy incinerators.
left.tooltip: Turn the garbage mountains into megawatts.
left.reveal: The dumps shrink and the grid gains power. The air near the plants worsens and the waste-pickers lose their livelihood.

right.label: Invest in recycling and the informal sector.
right.tooltip: The pickers are a workforce, not a problem.
right.reveal: You formalise recycling and protect the pickers' living. Less spectacular than a power plant, and the dumps shrink slower.

speaker: Rooppur Waste Officer
prompt: The new nuclear plant will soon produce spent fuel with nowhere safe to go. Russia offers to take it back — at a price and a dependency — or you build a domestic store you have no experience to manage.

left.label: Ship the waste back to Russia.
left.tooltip: Let the builder keep its poison.
left.reveal: The spent fuel leaves on Russian terms. The cost is steep and the dependency on Moscow deepens with each cask.

right.label: Build a domestic storage facility.
right.tooltip: A nuclear nation must handle its own ash.
right.reveal: You commit to a homegrown store. It is a daunting first for your engineers, and a risk if mishandled, but it keeps you sovereign.

speaker: Tea Estate Labour Commissioner
prompt: The tea-garden workers — generations bonded to the estates on a pittance — are striking for a living wage. The estate owners, a colonial-era elite, warn that doubling the daily rate ruins the gardens.

left.label: Mandate a living wage in the gardens.
left.tooltip: A century of bondage is long enough.
left.reveal: The pickers win a wage they can live on and bless your name. The estate owners cut output and lobby furiously against you.

right.label: Side with the owners to protect output.
right.tooltip: Ruin the gardens and everyone loses.
right.reveal: Production holds and the owners stay sweet. The pickers return to their lines and their poverty, and remember who chose.

speaker: EV Transition Lead
prompt: Electric three-wheelers and bikes are multiplying, cutting fuel imports but overloading a grid that wasn't built for them and threatening the petrol-pump and CNG economy.

left.label: Embrace the EV surge with charging infrastructure.
left.tooltip: Electrify the streets and cut the fuel bill.
left.reveal: Fuel imports drop and the air improves. The strained grid groans and the fuel-retail economy contracts painfully.

right.label: Throttle EVs to protect the grid and fuel trade.
right.tooltip: The wires and the pumps cannot take the shock.
right.reveal: You slow the rollout to spare the grid and the pump owners. Fuel imports stay high and the cleaner future waits.

speaker: Forest Conservator
prompt: Organised gangs are felling the protected forests for timber and farmland, often with a forest officer's bribe. A real anti-logging operation means arming rangers against well-connected timber barons.

left.label: Arm the rangers and break the timber gangs.
left.tooltip: A felled forest is a flood let in.
left.reveal: The chainsaws fall silent under pressure and the canopy holds. The timber barons and their official partners turn dangerous.

right.label: Let the quiet felling continue.
right.tooltip: Do not war with the timber money.
right.reveal: The forests keep thinning at the edges. Each lost hectare is one less wall against the next cyclone surge.

speaker: Household Gas Connection Board
prompt: There is not enough gas for new household pipeline connections, yet millions on the waiting list — and your MPs — demand them. The board wants the moratorium held; the public wants the queue cleared.

left.label: Lift the moratorium and connect households.
left.tooltip: The people were promised the blue flame.
left.reveal: Families on the list finally get piped gas and cheer. The supply stretches thinner, pressure drops, and industry is starved.

right.label: Hold the moratorium, push bottled gas instead.
right.tooltip: I cannot pipe gas we do not have.
right.reveal: You steer households to cylinders and keep the pipeline for industry. The waiting millions grumble at the broken promise.

speaker: Renewables Target Auditor
prompt: You pledged an ambitious renewable-energy share by 2030 and are nowhere near it. The auditor says you can quietly water down the target, or pour real money into solar and wind to chase it honestly.

left.label: Quietly lower the renewable target.
left.tooltip: A missed pledge is worse than a modest one.
left.reveal: You revise the goal down before you miss it. The climate world notes the retreat and your green credibility dims.

right.label: Fund a real push to hit the target.
right.tooltip: I will meet the promise, not edit it.
right.reveal: You commit serious money to solar and wind. The budget strains, but the pledge — and the cleaner grid — start to look real.

speaker: Stone Quarry Inspector
prompt: Illegal stone extraction in the northeast is gutting riverbeds and hillsides, feeding the construction boom and the pockets of local strongmen. The inspector wants the quarries shut.

left.label: Shut the illegal quarries.
left.tooltip: A gutted hillside is a landslide waiting.
left.reveal: You halt the extraction and the rivers begin to settle. Construction-stone prices jump and the local bosses lose a racket and turn hostile.

right.label: Tolerate the quarrying for the building boom.
right.tooltip: The country is being built; it needs stone.
right.reveal: Construction roars on cheap stone. The hills slump, the rivers choke, and the strongmen stay loyal and rich.

speaker: Cross-Border Power Renegotiator
prompt: The dollar-indexed tariff on imported Indian electricity has ballooned as the taka fell, and a lopsided clause lets the seller pass on every cost. The renegotiator wants to reopen the contract — at the risk of the supplier pulling the plug.

left.label: Demand the contract be reopened.
left.tooltip: I will not bleed dollars on a rigged clause.
left.reveal: You force renegotiation and may claw back terms. The supplier threatens curtailment and a supply gap looms.

right.label: Honour the contract to keep the power flowing.
right.tooltip: A dark grid costs more than a bad clause.
right.reveal: The electricity keeps coming uninterrupted. The dollar-indexed bill keeps climbing and draining the reserves.

speaker: Coastal Salinity Officer
prompt: Saltwater shrimp farms are lucrative dollar-earners, but the brine they flood the land with is poisoning the soil and the drinking water of the whole coastal belt. Rice farmers and shrimp barons are at war.

left.label: Curb the shrimp farms, protect the freshwater.
left.tooltip: Salt money is killing the coast's water.
left.reveal: You roll back the brine and the soil and wells begin to recover. The shrimp barons — major exporters — lobby and resist.

right.label: Let the lucrative shrimp farms expand.
right.tooltip: The dollars from shrimp are real and now.
right.reveal: Export earnings rise on the back of the brine. The coastal belt's fields and drinking water grow saltier each season.

speaker: Atomic Energy Chairman
prompt: The Russian-built nuclear plant is nearly done, financed by a loan that will shadow the budget for decades. With the country under sanctions pressure for dealing with Moscow, the chairman asks whether to fuel it up on schedule or pause and renegotiate the terms.

left.label: Fuel it on schedule, debt and sanctions be damned.
left.tooltip: A finished reactor left cold is the costliest folly.
left.reveal: The plant comes online and adds clean baseload power. The repayment millstone settles on the budget, and the Russia tie draws cold stares from the West.

right.label: Pause and renegotiate the loan terms.
right.tooltip: A debt unexamined is a debt that devours.
right.reveal: You halt commissioning to renegotiate. The power is delayed and Moscow is irritated, but you claw back better terms and a little distance from the sanctions glare.

speaker: Mining Concession Director
prompt: A foreign company wants to open-pit mine the vast Phulbari coal seam — enough fuel for decades — but it would displace tens of thousands and drain the aquifer of a whole farming region. The last time it was proposed, three protesters were shot dead.

left.label: Approve the open-pit mine.
left.tooltip: A seam this rich cannot stay buried.
left.reveal: The contract is signed and the draglines arrive. Villages are marked for clearance, the protests reignite with the memory of the dead, and the region braces for a fight.

right.label: Refuse open-pit; pursue underground only.
right.tooltip: I will not drown a region to dig a hole.
right.reveal: You bar the open pit and limit any extraction to less-disruptive methods. The yield is smaller and the company walks, but the farmland and the aquifer survive.

speaker: Distribution Utility Chief
prompt: A fifth of the power you generate is stolen or lost — illegal hooks, bribed meter readers, crumbling lines. The chief can fund a hard crackdown with prepaid meters and disconnections, hitting the poor hardest, or absorb the loss and keep the peace.

left.label: Crack down hard with prepaid meters.
left.tooltip: Power given free bankrupts the power.
left.reveal: The losses fall and the books improve. The poorest, suddenly metered and disconnected, feel the squeeze, and the line workers lose a lucrative racket.

right.label: Phase it in gently, subsidise the poorest.
right.tooltip: A meter that starves a family is a riot in waiting.
right.reveal: You spread the reform over years with lifeline tariffs. The theft bleeds the utility longer, but no slum erupts over a dark Eid.

speaker: Energy Regulator
prompt: Imported fuel costs have blown a hole in the power subsidy. The regulator presents a steep tariff hike to plug it — politically toxic but fiscally vital — or you can hold prices and let the subsidy keep bleeding the treasury.

left.label: Raise the tariff to cover costs.
left.tooltip: Cheap power the state cannot afford is no gift.
left.reveal: The hike steadies the books and enrages every household and factory. The opposition makes the electricity bill its rallying cry for the season.

right.label: Hold the tariff, eat the subsidy.
right.tooltip: I will not light my own funeral pyre with a price rise.
right.reveal: Prices stay frozen and the public exhales. The subsidy gouges the budget deeper, crowding out the schools and clinics that might have been built.

speaker: Petrobangla Chairman
prompt: Domestic gas is running dry and a new floating LNG terminal would secure imports — but it locks the country into volatile world prices in dollars you do not have. The chairman wants the deal signed before winter shortages bite.

left.label: Sign the LNG terminal deal.
left.tooltip: A cold factory is worse than an expensive one.
left.reveal: The terminal is contracted and the supply secured. The country's energy now rides the global spot market, and every price spike abroad becomes a crisis at home.

right.label: Hold off; push domestic exploration first.
right.tooltip: Imported gas is a habit I cannot afford.
right.reveal: You delay the terminal and pour money into drilling at home. Winter will be tight and shortages likely, but you refuse to mortgage the grid to a foreign spot price.

speaker: Bapex Managing Director
prompt: The national exploration company swears it can find and develop new gas fields cheaply — if you fund it instead of handing the blocks to international oil majors on production-sharing terms that ship most of the gas value abroad.

left.label: Hand the blocks to the international majors.
left.tooltip: Foreign rigs find gas faster than national pride.
left.reveal: The majors move in with capital and speed. Gas flows sooner, but the production-sharing terms send the lion's share of the value out of the country.

right.label: Fund Bapex to develop the blocks itself.
right.tooltip: Gas drawn by our own hands stays in our own pockets.
right.reveal: You back the national company. Development is slower and riskier, but a strike would belong wholly to the nation, building skill that outlasts any contract.

speaker: Irrigation Authority Head
prompt: The deep tube-wells that made the parched Barind tract bloom with rice are draining the aquifer faster than the rains refill it. The head warns of a slow-motion desert; the farmers warn that any cut to pumping means a failed harvest now.

left.label: Keep the pumps running at full tilt.
left.tooltip: A failed harvest topples a government faster than a dry decade.
left.reveal: The rice keeps coming and the farmers stay calm. The water table sinks another year toward the day the wells suck only dust.

right.label: Cap pumping, shift to drought-hardy crops.
right.tooltip: A region that mines its own water has a deadline.
right.reveal: You ration the aquifer and push less-thirsty crops. This season's yields dip and the farmers grumble, but the Barind may still have water in a generation.

speaker: Environment Inspector
prompt: The leather tanneries, relocated to a new estate, still dump untreated chromium effluent straight into the river because the promised treatment plant was never finished. The inspector wants them shut until it works; the exporters threaten to flee the country.

left.label: Let the tanneries keep running.
left.tooltip: Leather earns dollars the river cannot.
left.reveal: The exports continue and the chromium flows. A whole river system and the people who drink from it pay the toll, and the unbuilt treatment plant stays a fiction.

right.label: Shut them until the treatment plant works.
right.tooltip: A river is not a sewer for export earnings.
right.reveal: You close the estate until it cleans up. Exporters rage and some relocate abroad, but the river gets a chance and the rule finally has teeth.

speaker: Ports Development Chairman
prompt: A deep-sea port would transform trade — and three different foreign backers (one Japanese, one Chinese, one Indian) are competing to build it, each deal carrying strategic strings. The chairman needs you to pick a patron, or build it slower on your own dime.

left.label: Take the cheapest foreign deal and its strings.
left.tooltip: A port now is worth a patron's leash.
left.reveal: Construction races ahead on foreign money. The port rises fast, and so does the geopolitical debt — the chosen patron now has a foothold on your coast.

right.label: Build slower, financed at home.
right.tooltip: A harbour bought with strings docks foreign warships.
right.reveal: You forgo the fast foreign money for a slower, self-funded build. The port is years away, but no rival power gains a berth in your strategic waters.

speaker: District Mineral Officer
prompt: In the northeast, illegal stone quarrying is gutting the riverbeds and hill slopes, feeding the construction boom but collapsing banks and killing labourers in pit cave-ins. The officer asks whether to enforce the ban or keep the cheap aggregate flowing.

left.label: Let the quarrying continue.
left.tooltip: The cities are built on this stone.
left.reveal: The aggregate keeps the construction sector cheap and humming. The rivers braid into ruin, the slopes slump, and another pit swallows another crew.

right.label: Enforce the quarrying ban.
right.tooltip: Stone pulled from a living river kills it.
right.reveal: You shut the illegal pits and the riverbeds begin to heal. Construction costs tick up and the local mafias lose a fortune, sending their muscle against your officers.

speaker: Fisheries Director
prompt: The national fish spawns only if you enforce a strict fishing ban during the breeding season — but that means tens of thousands of fishing families with no income for weeks. The director can enforce the ban hard, or look away to spare the hunger.

left.label: Enforce the breeding-season ban hard.
left.tooltip: A fish that cannot spawn feeds no one next year.
left.reveal: Patrols keep the nets out of the water and the stocks rebound spectacularly. The fishing families go hungry for the weeks of the ban, and resent every patrol boat.

right.label: Relax the ban to spare the families.
right.tooltip: A starving fisherman cannot wait for next season.
right.reveal: You ease enforcement and the families eat. The catch this season holds, but the spawning falters and next year's nets come up lighter.

speaker: Renewable Energy Adviser
prompt: A net-metering scheme would let factories and homes put rooftop solar on the grid and sell back the surplus — cutting imports and emissions. The utilities resist, since every rooftop panel is a customer they lose. The adviser asks whose side you take.

left.label: Side with the utilities, stall net metering.
left.tooltip: A grid undercut by a million rooftops collapses.
left.reveal: You protect the utilities' monopoly and the rooftops stay bare. Fuel imports stay high, and the clean-energy future drifts another few years out of reach.

right.label: Mandate generous net metering.
right.tooltip: Every rooftop is a power plant I did not have to build.
right.reveal: You force the grid open to rooftop solar. The utilities howl about lost revenue, but imports ease, the air clears, and a solar industry takes root.

speaker: Grid Interconnection Officer
prompt: You can import cheap hydropower across the border through a regional grid — clean, plentiful, and a hedge against fuel shocks. But it deepens dependence on a giant neighbour who could, in a quarrel, simply pull the plug.

left.label: Sign the cross-border power import.
left.tooltip: Cheap clean power outweighs a distant risk.
left.reveal: The hydropower flows in and bills ease. Your grid now leans on a neighbour's goodwill, a switch in a foreign capital that could one day darken your cities.

right.label: Decline; build domestic capacity instead.
right.tooltip: A switch in another's hand is a noose in waiting.
right.reveal: You forgo the cheap import for self-reliance. Power stays costlier and dirtier for now, but no foreign hand rests on your grid's master switch.

speaker: Agriculture Minister
prompt: The state fertilizer plants devour scarce gas to make cheap urea for farmers — gas the power sector is desperate for. The minister asks whether to keep feeding the fertilizer plants for food security, or divert the gas to keep the lights on.

left.label: Keep gas flowing to fertilizer.
left.tooltip: Cheap urea is cheap rice is a calm country.
left.reveal: The farmers get their subsidised urea and the harvest holds. The power sector starves of the diverted gas, and load-shedding creeps back into the cities.

right.label: Divert the gas to power generation.
right.tooltip: A dark grid stops a country faster than dear fertilizer.
right.reveal: You redirect the gas to the turbines and the lights stay on. Fertilizer prices climb, farmers protest, and the next harvest carries a question mark.

speaker: Power Cell Director
prompt: The notorious law that lets you award power-plant contracts with no tender and no legal challenge — the source of the ruinous 'capacity charges' paid to idle plants — is up for renewal. The director's friends in the rental business want it extended again.

left.label: Renew the no-tender indemnity law.
left.tooltip: My donors' plants must keep their guarantees.
left.reveal: The law is extended and the capacity charges keep flowing to idle plants. Your rental-baron donors are delighted, and the public keeps paying for power it never receives.

right.label: Let the indemnity law lapse.
right.tooltip: Paying for electricity that never comes is a scandal I will end.
right.reveal: You let the shield expire and open the sector to tenders and lawsuits. The rental barons rage and threaten, but the rip-off capacity charges finally face daylight.

speaker: Environment Inspector
prompt: Thousands of illegal brick kilns ring the cities, burning coal and the country's fertile topsoil into smog that chokes the capital every winter. The inspector wants them forced onto clean technology or shut; the kiln owners employ a million workers.

left.label: Leave the kilns as they are.
left.tooltip: A million jobs outweigh a season of smog.
left.reveal: The kilns keep firing and the workers keep their wages. The capital's winter air becomes the world's worst, and the cropland loses its skin one kiln at a time.

right.label: Force clean kilns or closure.
right.tooltip: Bricks baked from topsoil and smog cost more than they earn.
right.reveal: You mandate cleaner zigzag technology and shut the worst offenders. Some kilns close and workers are displaced, but the air and the farmland get a reprieve.

speaker: Coast Guard Commander
prompt: Foreign industrial trawlers are sweeping your newly-won maritime zone bare, often with bribed permits, while artisanal fishers come home empty. The commander can mount costly patrols to drive them out, or keep taking the licence fees and look away.

left.label: Keep the licence fees, look away.
left.tooltip: The fees are certain; the patrols are expensive.
left.reveal: The foreign trawlers keep dragging your waters and the fees fill a ledger. The small fishers' nets come up empty, and the fishery heads toward collapse.

right.label: Patrol hard and expel the trawlers.
right.tooltip: A sea swept bare by foreigners feeds no one here.
right.reveal: You fund the patrols and drive the foreign fleets out. It costs the navy dearly and angers the licence-buyers' governments, but the stocks and the artisanal fishers recover.

speaker: Industries Secretary
prompt: The country has become a dumping ground for the world's electronic waste, picked apart by hand in toxic backyard yards — including children's. The secretary can crack down and demand a formal recycling industry, or keep the cheap scrap trade flowing.

left.label: Let the informal e-waste trade run.
left.tooltip: Scrap is scrap, and it employs the poor.
left.reveal: The backyard yards keep smelting circuit boards over open fires. The lead and cadmium seep into the children who work them, and the world keeps shipping its poison here.

right.label: Mandate a formal recycling industry.
right.tooltip: I will not let the world poison our children for scrap.
right.reveal: You ban the backyard trade and license proper recyclers. The cheap scrap economy contracts, but the toxic exposure falls and a real industry begins to form.

speaker: Forest Conservator
prompt: To protect the mangrove forest you can bar the honey-collectors, woodcutters, and fishers whose families have lived off it for centuries — or let them keep harvesting and watch the forest thin. The conservator says the forest cannot bear both protection and people forever.

left.label: Bar the forest communities for conservation.
left.tooltip: A forest emptied of axes survives.
left.reveal: You evict the harvesters and the mangroves recover. Generations of forest families lose their only livelihood, and resentment festers along the forest's edge.

right.label: Let the communities keep harvesting sustainably.
right.tooltip: A forest is kept by those who live in it, not those who fence it.
right.reveal: You license sustainable, limited harvesting and bring the communities into protection. The forest thins more slowly, and its people become its guardians instead of its enemies.

speaker: Jute Ministry Secretary
prompt: The state jute mills — once the 'golden fibre' that built the economy — bleed losses every year, kept alive for the workers and the heritage. The secretary can shut the rotting mills to stop the bleeding, or pour in another bailout and a modernisation gamble.

left.label: Shut the loss-making jute mills.
left.tooltip: Nostalgia is not a balance sheet.
left.reveal: You close the mills and stem the losses. Tens of thousands of workers and a piece of national identity are cast off, and the jute towns turn against you.

right.label: Bail out and modernise the mills.
right.tooltip: The golden fibre may yet be gold again.
right.reveal: You fund a modernisation push toward jute textiles and bioplastics. It is a costly gamble, but the workers keep their looms and a heritage industry gets one more chance.

speaker: Coal Import Coordinator
prompt: Your new coal plants need a steady stream of imported coal, and a dedicated deep-water terminal would lock in cheap, reliable supply for decades. It also chains the country to coal long after the world has moved to phase it out — and to financing it.

left.label: Build the dedicated coal terminal.
left.tooltip: My plants are built; they must be fed.
left.reveal: The terminal secures decades of cheap coal for the plants you have. You also build infrastructure the world is racing to abandon, a stranded asset waiting to happen.

right.label: Cancel the terminal, plan for coal's exit.
right.tooltip: A terminal for a dying fuel is a tomb I am paying to dig.
right.reveal: You scrap the terminal and begin planning coal's phase-out. The existing plants run short-fed and costly, but you stop sinking money into a stranded future.

speaker: WASA Managing Director
prompt: The capital's piped water is so contaminated that millions boil it or buy it by the jar, while the rivers ringing the city are biologically dead. The MD needs a vast, expensive overhaul of treatment and sewerage — or you can keep patching and let people cope.

left.label: Keep patching the old system.
left.tooltip: A full overhaul is a budget I do not have.
left.reveal: You make do with repairs and the taps keep running brown. The dead rivers stay dead, the bottled-water bills crush the poor, and waterborne disease simmers on.

right.label: Fund the full water and sewerage overhaul.
right.tooltip: A capital that cannot drink its own water is failing.
right.reveal: You commit to the costly rebuild of treatment and sewers. The budget groans for years, but a generation may finally drink from the tap without fear.

speaker: Petrobangla Chairman
prompt: A foreign operator's well has blown out, burning a forest and a gas reserve worth a fortune for weeks. The company offers a token settlement and a denial of liability. The chairman asks whether to fight for full compensation or take the quiet money and move on.

left.label: Take the token settlement quietly.
left.tooltip: A long fight scares off the next investor.
left.reveal: You accept the pittance and sign away the claim. The reserve and the forest are gone for nothing, and every future operator learns that recklessness here is cheap.

right.label: Demand full compensation in court.
right.tooltip: A burned reserve is a debt the burner will pay.
right.reveal: You sue for the true value of the lost gas and the scorched land. The investor climate cools and the case drags, but you set a precedent that negligence here has a price.

speaker: Energy Secretary
prompt: With gas reserves dwindling, you have frozen new household pipeline connections — but millions on the waiting list, who have paid bribes for years, are furious. The secretary can lift the freeze to win goodwill, or hold it to ration the dying reserve for industry.

left.label: Lift the freeze, connect the households.
left.tooltip: A promised connection denied is a vote lost.
left.reveal: The taps open for the waiting millions and the goodwill flows. The reserve drains faster toward the day the gas simply runs out, for homes and factories alike.

right.label: Hold the freeze, ration gas for industry.
right.tooltip: Gas burned in a stove builds nothing; gas in a factory builds a country.
right.reveal: You keep the freeze and steer the gas to industry and power. The waiting millions seethe, but the reserve stretches further and the factories keep running.

speaker: Bridges Authority Chairman
prompt: A signature river tunnel has opened, financed by foreign loans, but traffic is a fraction of the rosy forecasts that justified it. The chairman can set punishing tolls to service the debt, or keep tolls low and let the loss sit on the public books.

left.label: Set high tolls to service the debt.
left.tooltip: The users of the tunnel should pay for it.
left.reveal: The tolls bite and the debt is serviced. Hauliers reroute to avoid the cost, traffic thins further, and the showpiece becomes a monument to optimistic forecasts.

right.label: Keep tolls low, absorb the loss.
right.tooltip: A tunnel no one can afford to use is a tomb.
right.reveal: You keep the toll cheap and the traffic builds. The debt sits unpaid on the public ledger, a quiet weight that some future budget will have to carry.

speaker: Transport Electrification Adviser
prompt: Imported fuel for vehicles drains your dollars by the billion. The adviser proposes aggressive incentives for electric vehicles and a charging network — slashing the oil bill over time, but stranding the gas-station economy and demanding more grid power up front.

left.label: Push hard for the EV transition.
left.tooltip: Every electric mile is a dollar that stays home.
left.reveal: Incentives and chargers roll out and the EVs come. The fuel-import bill begins its long decline, while the grid strains and the petrol-pump economy starts to wither.

right.label: Stick with the existing fuel fleet.
right.tooltip: A grid this fragile cannot charge a nation's cars.
right.reveal: You hold off and keep the petrol economy intact. The fuel-import bill keeps bleeding dollars, but the strained grid is spared and the pump-workers keep their trade.

speaker: Renewable Energy Adviser
prompt: The coast has strong, steady wind, and a foreign developer offers to build a large wind farm — clean power, but on prime coastal land that fishers and salt-farmers use, and at a guaranteed tariff higher than coal.

left.label: Approve the coastal wind farm.
left.tooltip: Wind is the one fuel no one can embargo.
left.reveal: The turbines rise and clean power flows. The fishers and salt-farmers lose ground and the tariff is steep, but the country gains a fuel free of import and emission.

right.label: Reject it to protect the coastal commons.
right.tooltip: A green plant on stolen land is not clean.
right.reveal: You refuse to displace the coastal livelihoods for the turbines. The clean-power gain is forgone, but the fishers and salt-farmers keep the shore they have always worked.

speaker: Land Reform Commissioner
prompt: New river islands — fertile silt 'chars' — have surfaced, and they are state land by law. The commissioner can distribute them to the landless families who already farm them, or let the local strongmen who 'manage' the chars formalise their grab.

left.label: Let the strongmen formalise their hold.
left.tooltip: The chars are already governed by those who hold them.
left.reveal: The mastans get their titles and the landless become their tenants. A vast tract of fertile new land entrenches the very feudalism land reform was meant to end.

right.label: Distribute the chars to the landless.
right.tooltip: New land should make new owners, not new landlords.
right.reveal: You title the chars to the families who farm them. The strongmen lose a prize and send their thugs, but thousands of landless households gain ground of their own.

speaker: Wetlands Director
prompt: The vast northeastern haor wetlands — a single boro rice crop, a fishery, a bird sanctuary — are being diked and converted to year-round farming, which raises yields but destroys the flood-pulse the whole ecosystem depends on. The director begs you to choose.

left.label: Allow the diking for higher yields.
left.tooltip: More crops from the same water is progress.
left.reveal: The dikes go up and the rice doubles for a while. The fishery crashes, the birds vanish, and one bad flash-flood now drowns a crop the wetland used to absorb.

right.label: Protect the natural flood-pulse.
right.tooltip: A wetland drained is a famine deferred.
right.reveal: You halt the conversion and protect the flood-pulse. The single-crop yields stay modest, but the fishery, the birds, and the wetland's flood defence all survive.

speaker: Tourism Board Chair
prompt: The country's only coral island is being loved to death — hotels on the dunes, day-trippers trampling the reef, plastic everywhere. The chair can cap visitors and tear down illegal resorts to save the reef, or keep the tourist revenue flowing.

left.label: Keep the tourism flowing.
left.tooltip: The island earns its keep in tourist taka.
left.reveal: The hotels stay and the boats keep coming. The reef bleaches and breaks under the crowds, and the one coral ecosystem in the country slides toward death.

right.label: Cap visitors, demolish illegal resorts.
right.tooltip: A dead reef draws no one.
right.reveal: You ration access and raze the illegal hotels. The local tourist economy takes a blow and the resort-owners sue, but the reef gets a fighting chance to recover.

speaker: Environment Secretary
prompt: Single-use plastic chokes every drain, river, and field in the country — and clogs the very canals whose blockage floods the capital. The secretary proposes a hard ban and a jute-bag mandate. The plastics industry, a big employer and donor, will fight to the death.

left.label: Hold off on the plastics ban.
left.tooltip: A ban that kills an industry kills jobs and donors.
left.reveal: You spare the plastics industry and the bags keep flowing. The drains clog, the floods worsen, and the microplastics work their way into the fish and the people.

right.label: Enforce the ban, mandate jute bags.
right.tooltip: A drain choked with plastic drowns a city.
right.reveal: You ban the worst single-use plastics and revive the jute-bag trade. The plastics lobby rages and some jobs vanish, but the drains clear and a jute revival stirs.

speaker: Geological Survey Director
prompt: The coastal beaches hold valuable heavy-mineral sands — zircon, titanium, rare earths — that a foreign miner wants to extract. It would earn real money, but strip-mining the beaches would gut the storm-buffering dunes and the turtle nesting grounds.

left.label: Approve the beach mineral mining.
left.tooltip: Rare earths in the sand are riches wasted unmined.
left.reveal: The miners move in and the export earnings begin. The dunes that blunt the cyclones are carried away by the truckload, and the coast loses its natural armour.

right.label: Keep the beaches and dunes intact.
right.tooltip: Dunes that stop a cyclone are worth more than zircon.
right.reveal: You refuse the concession. The mineral wealth stays buried, but the dunes keep standing between the storms and the people behind them.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: The coastal embankments that hold back the tides are decades old and failing; one big surge could drown a district. The chief needs a massive repair programme — or you can keep doing emergency patch-jobs after each breach, cheaper now and catastrophic later.

left.label: Keep patching breaches as they come.
left.tooltip: A full rebuild is a budget I will spend on the next disaster.
left.reveal: You fund only the emergency repairs. Each cyclone breaches the weak embankments anew, salting fields and drowning homes the rebuild would have saved.

right.label: Fund the full embankment rebuild.
right.tooltip: An embankment is cheap next to the district it saves.
right.reveal: You commit to rebuilding the polders properly. The cost is huge and slow to show, but a coastal district gains a real wall against the rising, angrier sea.

speaker: Industries Secretary
prompt: The construction boom runs on cement made from imported clinker and limestone, draining dollars. A domestic limestone quarry in the hills could cut the import bill — at the cost of blasting a forested range the indigenous communities hold sacred.

left.label: Open the domestic limestone quarry.
left.tooltip: A boom this big cannot run on imports forever.
left.reveal: The quarry opens and the cement-import bill eases. The sacred range is blasted apart, the indigenous communities are displaced, and another grievance hardens in the hills.

right.label: Keep importing, spare the sacred range.
right.tooltip: Some hills are worth more standing than crushed.
right.reveal: You leave the range intact and keep paying for imports. The dollar drain continues, but the forest and the communities who revere it are spared the dynamite.

speaker: Fisheries Director
prompt: The booming farmed-fish and shrimp sector dopes its ponds with banned antibiotics, and Europe is threatening to reject the exports over residue limits. The director can crack down hard — costly and unpopular with farmers — or keep certifying and hope the testing misses it.

left.label: Keep certifying, hope the tests miss it.
left.tooltip: A crackdown now empties a thousand ponds.
left.reveal: You keep the exports flowing on a wink. One bad batch detected abroad triggers a blanket ban, and a whole export sector collapses overnight on a gamble.

right.label: Crack down on the banned antibiotics.
right.tooltip: An export caught dirty loses the whole market.
right.reveal: You enforce the residue limits hard. The farmers howl at the cost and the disease losses, but the exports clear European labs and the market stays open.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: The country's rivers — its cheapest highways — are silting up, stranding the cargo boats that move grain and goods. A vast dredging programme would reopen them, but the dredged spoil has to go somewhere, and the cheapest dumping ruins floodplains and fisheries.

left.label: Dredge fast and dump spoil cheaply.
left.tooltip: Open rivers move a nation; tidy disposal can wait.
left.reveal: The channels reopen and the cargo flows again. The carelessly dumped spoil buries floodplains and chokes fisheries, trading one waterway's life for another's.

right.label: Dredge carefully with managed spoil.
right.tooltip: A river reopened by ruining another is no gain.
right.reveal: You dredge with proper spoil management and floodplain protection. It is slower and dearer, but the rivers reopen without killing the wetlands beside them.

speaker: Petrobangla Chairman
prompt: With the maritime boundary settled, you can finally open the deep offshore blocks to international bidding. The majors want generous, low-royalty terms to take the deep-water risk. The chairman asks how hard to bargain — gas in hand, or the nation's full share.

left.label: Offer generous terms to attract the majors.
left.tooltip: Gas undiscovered earns nothing; bring the rigs.
left.reveal: The sweet terms draw the majors and the drilling begins. Gas may flow within years, but the low royalties mean the nation pockets a thin slice of its own seabed.

right.label: Bargain hard for the nation's full share.
right.tooltip: Our seabed, our terms.
right.reveal: You hold out for higher royalties and faster work commitments. Some bidders walk and exploration slows, but any gas found will pay the nation what it is worth.

speaker: Commerce Secretary
prompt: A glut has crashed the price of locally-farmed salt, ruining the coastal salt-farmers, while industry lobbies to import cheap foreign salt for its factories. The secretary asks whether to protect the farmers with an import ban or let the cheap salt in.

left.label: Allow the cheap salt imports.
left.tooltip: Industry runs on the cheapest input.
left.reveal: The factories get their cheap salt and the lobby is pleased. The coastal salt-farmers, undercut to ruin, abandon the pans, and a coastal livelihood dies out.

right.label: Ban imports to protect the salt-farmers.
right.tooltip: A coast of ruined salt-pans is a coast of empty hands.
right.reveal: You shut out the foreign salt and prop up the local price. Industry grumbles at the cost, but the salt-farmers keep their pans and their precarious living.

speaker: Forest Conservator
prompt: To hit your 'green coverage' targets cheaply, the department wants to blanket cleared hills with fast-growing acacia and eucalyptus monoculture — green on paper, but a desert for wildlife that sucks the water table dry. The alternative is slower native-species restoration.

left.label: Plant the fast monoculture for green targets.
left.tooltip: A number on a chart is a number won.
left.reveal: The hills go green with acacia and the coverage statistics soar. Beneath the uniform canopy nothing lives, and the water table drops under the thirsty exotics.

right.label: Restore slow native forest instead.
right.tooltip: A plantation is not a forest.
right.reveal: You fund mixed native restoration. The 'green coverage' rises slowly and the targets slip, but a real forest — water, wildlife, and all — begins to return.

speaker: Petrobangla Chairman
prompt: Exploration has struck a major new onshore gas field — a genuine windfall. The chairman asks how to use it: burn it fast and cheap to win an easy few years of plenty, or husband it as a long-term reserve and feedstock for industry.

left.label: Burn it fast for a few easy years.
left.tooltip: Plenty now buys loyalty now.
left.reveal: You open the taps and the country enjoys cheap, abundant gas. The field empties in a handful of years, and the windfall is gone before it built anything lasting.

right.label: Husband it as a long-term reserve.
right.tooltip: A field burned in five years is five years of folly.
right.reveal: You meter the new field for industry and the long haul. The public sees no instant bonanza, but the reserve underwrites a decade of factories and fertiliser.

speaker: Power Development Chairman
prompt: The coal plant beside the mangrove forest is running, and its backers now want a second, larger unit on the same site. The chairman calls it cheap megawatts on proven ground; the scientists warn it doubles the threat to the forest the country shelters behind.

left.label: Approve the second coal unit.
left.tooltip: Proven ground builds the cheapest megawatts.
left.reveal: The expansion is greenlit and the capacity doubles. So does the ash, the warm effluent, and the shipping traffic gnawing at the forest's edge.

right.label: Cap the site, build clean capacity elsewhere.
right.tooltip: I will not double a bet against the forest.
right.reveal: You forbid the second unit and steer new capacity to cleaner sites. The cheap megawatts are forgone, but the mangrove shield is not made to carry twice the load.

speaker: Hill Resources Officer
prompt: A survey has found valuable minerals under indigenous land in the hill tracts. The communities, citing the peace accord, demand consent and a share; the mining lobby says national resources belong to the nation, accord or not. The officer awaits your call.

left.label: Mine without indigenous consent.
left.tooltip: The nation's minerals are the nation's, not a clan's.
left.reveal: You authorise extraction over the communities' objections. The minerals flow and so does the fury; the peace accord, already brittle, cracks a little wider.

right.label: Require consent and a community share.
right.tooltip: Wealth dug from a people's land owes that people.
right.reveal: You make extraction conditional on consent and royalties to the communities. The lobby fumes at the precedent, but the hills, for once, share in their own ground's wealth.

speaker: Irrigation Authority Head
prompt: Free and unmetered irrigation power has farmers pumping groundwater with abandon. The head proposes prepaid meters on the irrigation pumps to curb the waste and the subsidy bleed — but a metered pump in a bad season is a farmer's ruin.

left.label: Meter the irrigation pumps.
left.tooltip: Water and power given free are water and power wasted.
left.reveal: The meters go on and the reckless pumping eases, sparing the aquifer and the subsidy. In the dry years, though, a metered pump becomes one more cost a failing farmer cannot meet.

right.label: Keep irrigation free and unmetered.
right.tooltip: A meter on a farmer's pump is a meter on his harvest.
right.reveal: You leave the pumps free and the farmers easy. The aquifer keeps falling and the subsidy keeps bleeding, but no farmer loses a crop to a card with no credit on it.

speaker: Labour Inspector
prompt: The ship-breaking beaches that feed the country's steel are death-traps — no safety gear, asbestos, men cut in half by falling plate, child labour at the torches. The inspector demands enforced standards; the yard owners say the margins cannot bear them.

left.label: Leave the yards to their own ways.
left.tooltip: Cheap steel needs cheap hands.
left.reveal: The yards keep running on blood and asbestos. The steel stays cheap, the casualty list grows, and the next exposé brands the beaches the world's most lethal workplace.

right.label: Enforce hard safety standards.
right.tooltip: Steel bought with a man a week is too dear.
right.reveal: You force protective gear, asbestos controls, and an end to child labour. Margins thin and some yards close, but men stop dying by the dozen on the tide line.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: Engineers propose a colossal canal to divert water from the wet south to the drought-prone north — a nation-reshaping project. The chief calls it a permanent answer to the north's thirst; ecologists warn it could wreck the river systems at both ends.

left.label: Greenlight the grand canal.
left.tooltip: A nation that moves its own water masters its fate.
left.reveal: The mega-project launches to fanfare and a flood of contracts. The drought-prone north dreams of water, while ecologists count the rivers the canal will starve and drown.

right.label: Shelve it for smaller, local schemes.
right.tooltip: A canal that breaks two rivers to fill one is folly.
right.reveal: You set aside the grand scheme for modest local water projects. The headline-grabbing fix is gone, but the rivers keep their flow and the money spreads wider.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: You pressed ahead, and the canal's upstream intake has dried the wetlands and fisheries of the donor basin, igniting protests there even as the north celebrates its new water. The donor districts demand the intake be throttled back.

left.label: Keep the intake at full flow for the north.
left.tooltip: The north's thirst was the whole point.
left.reveal: You hold the flow and the north blooms. The donor basin's fisheries collapse and its people, betrayed, turn the canal into a symbol of one region drained for another.

right.label: Throttle the intake to spare the donor basin.
right.tooltip: I will not drown one region to water another.
right.reveal: You cut the diversion back to a sustainable flow. The north's gains shrink and its boosters grumble, but the donor basin's wetlands and fishers are pulled back from ruin.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: With the canal half-built and half-cursed, the contractors — politically connected — demand the full budget regardless of the scaled-back flow, threatening to abandon the works and sue. You can pay them off or face a stalled, litigated ruin in the landscape.

left.label: Pay the contractors in full.
left.tooltip: A half-dug canal is worse than none.
left.reveal: You pay out to keep the works moving. The connected contractors pocket the windfall, the budget bleeds, and the project limps on under a cloud of inflated bills.

right.label: Hold them to the scaled-back contract.
right.tooltip: I will not be extorted by my own diggers.
right.reveal: You refuse the padded claim and renegotiate to the reduced scope. The contractors threaten and litigate, but the public is spared paying full price for half a canal.

speaker: Grid Operations Chief
prompt: The national grid is so brittle that a single fault can — and once did — black out the entire country at once. The chief wants a costly redesign with regional islanding and redundancy; the money is wanted elsewhere until the next nationwide blackout.

left.label: Fund the grid-resilience redesign.
left.tooltip: A grid that fails all at once fails the whole nation at once.
left.reveal: You fund islanding, redundancy, and modern controls. The cost is heavy and the benefit invisible between failures, but a single fault can no longer plunge the entire country into darkness.

right.label: Patch the grid and hope.
right.tooltip: A full redesign is a budget for a disaster that may not come.
right.reveal: You make do with patches. The money goes elsewhere, the brittle grid stays brittle, and the country waits, one bad fault away from another total nationwide blackout.

speaker: Rural Electrification Chief
prompt: Remote villages can be reached either by costly grid extension or by the off-grid solar home systems the country pioneered. The chief asks whether to push solar to the last villages now, or wait years to bring them the grid that powers heavier loads.

left.label: Push off-grid solar to the last villages.
left.tooltip: Light tonight beats a grid in a decade.
left.reveal: You blanket the remote villages with solar home systems. Millions get clean light and phone-charging now, though the small panels cannot run the mills and pumps a grid eventually would.

right.label: Wait and extend the full grid.
right.tooltip: A village deserves real power, not a panel.
right.reveal: You hold out to bring the villages the full grid. They wait years longer in darkness, but when the lines arrive they carry the heavy loads that solar panels never could.

speaker: Power Transition Adviser
prompt: Pressure mounts to retire the ageing, dirty coal plants ahead of schedule. Doing so cleans the air and the country's image, but strands billions in assets and throws thousands of plant and mine workers out of work in towns built around the coal.

left.label: Retire the coal plants with a just transition.
left.tooltip: A dying industry should be eased to rest, not abandoned.
left.reveal: You retire the plants early and fund retraining and new industry for the coal towns. The stranded-asset cost is steep, but the air clears, the image brightens, and the workers are not simply discarded.

right.label: Keep the coal plants running to term.
right.tooltip: I will not strand billions and a workforce for an early exit.
right.reveal: You keep the coal plants running to their full life. The assets and jobs are preserved, the towns stay employed, and the dirty smoke and the climate criticism keep mounting year on year.

speaker: Energy Security Director
prompt: The country holds only days of fuel stock, leaving it dangerously exposed to any supply shock or blockade. The director wants a costly strategic petroleum reserve and bigger storage depots; the capital is large and the threat feels abstract in calm times.

left.label: Build a strategic fuel reserve.
left.tooltip: A nation with days of fuel is a nation days from paralysis.
left.reveal: You build the strategic reserve and expand storage. The capital outlay is large, but the next supply shock or blockade meets a buffer instead of an instant fuel crisis.

right.label: Run on thin fuel stocks.
right.tooltip: A reserve is a fortune frozen against a maybe.
right.reveal: You keep stocks thin to save the capital. The money stays free for other needs, and the country stays days away from paralysis should any supply shock or blockade come.

speaker: Energy Access Director
prompt: Most rural households still cook on smoky biomass, ruining women's lungs and the forests. A push to make bottled LPG cheap and available would change that — through subsidy and a safe distribution network, or you can leave the woodsmoke as it is.

left.label: Subsidise rural LPG access.
left.tooltip: A woman's lungs are worth more than the price of a cylinder.
left.reveal: You subsidise LPG and build safe distribution. Woodsmoke recedes from kitchens, women's health improves and the forests get a reprieve, and the subsidy and cylinder-safety burden fall on the budget.

right.label: Leave rural cooking to biomass.
right.tooltip: An LPG subsidy is a cost the budget cannot carry.
right.reveal: You leave rural kitchens to firewood and dung. The budget is spared, and women keep breathing woodsmoke into ruined lungs while the forests keep falling for fuel.

speaker: Energy Diplomacy Director
prompt: A regional gas pipeline could bring steady, cheaper gas across borders for decades — energy security of a kind — but it threads through and depends on neighbours' goodwill, and chains your grid to a fuel and a transit route others control.

left.label: Commit to the cross-border gas pipeline.
left.tooltip: Steady gas for decades is worth a careful diplomacy.
left.reveal: You commit to the pipeline and cheaper gas begins to flow. Your energy security improves and your dependence deepens, your grid now hostage to a transit route and a neighbour's goodwill.

right.label: Invest in domestic supply instead.
right.tooltip: A pipeline through a neighbour is a valve in his hand.
right.reveal: You forgo the pipeline for domestic exploration and LNG. The gas is costlier and slower, but no foreign hand rests on the valve that feeds your grid.

speaker: Atomic Energy Chairman
prompt: The new nuclear plant will soon produce spent fuel that must be stored safely for generations, and no permanent solution exists. The chairman can fund a proper long-term storage and safety regime now, or rely on the foreign builder's vague take-back promise and hope.

left.label: Fund proper long-term waste storage.
left.tooltip: A poison that lasts ten thousand years deserves a plan.
left.reveal: You invest in genuine spent-fuel storage and safety. The cost is large and the benefit unseen for decades, but you do not leave a radioactive legacy to be improvised by your grandchildren.

right.label: Rely on the builder's take-back promise.
right.tooltip: The Russians said they would handle it; let them.
right.reveal: You lean on the foreign builder's vague assurances and defer the problem. The money is saved now, and a growing pile of spent fuel waits on a promise no contract firmly guarantees.

speaker: Power Regulator
prompt: Big factories run their own gas-fired captive power plants, abandoning the grid because it is unreliable — which starves the grid of its best customers and worsens it for everyone else. The regulator can force them back onto the grid, or let industry self-supply.

left.label: Force big industry back onto the grid.
left.tooltip: A grid the strong abandon collapses on the weak.
left.reveal: You compel the factories to draw from the grid and surrender their gas. The grid's finances and reliability improve for all, and the industrialists rage at losing their dependable captive power.

right.label: Let industry keep captive power.
right.tooltip: A factory that cannot trust the grid will make its own.
right.reveal: You let the factories self-supply. Industry keeps its reliable power, and the grid, abandoned by its best customers, grows weaker and dearer for the households and small firms left on it.

speaker: Agriculture Research Director
prompt: A genetically-modified pest-resistant crop could cut pesticide use and lift yields, but activists warn of seed-company dependence and unknown risks, and the public is uneasy. The director asks whether to approve and promote it or hold back.

left.label: Approve and promote the GM crop.
left.tooltip: A crop that needs no poison feeds more for less.
left.reveal: You approve the GM variety and yields rise as pesticide use falls. The activists protest seed dependence and unknown risks, and farmers gain a hardier crop and a tie to the seed company that owns it.

right.label: Hold back on the GM crop.
right.tooltip: I will not bet the food supply on an untested gene.
right.reveal: You decline to approve it, citing precaution. The pesticide-heavy status quo persists and yields stay flat, but the food supply is not staked on a technology the public distrusts and a seed firm controls.

speaker: Coastal Afforestation Chief
prompt: A planted mangrove greenbelt along the coast would blunt cyclones and storm surges and nurse fisheries — but the land is coveted for shrimp farms and salt pans, and the planting is slow and easily encroached. The chief asks whether to commit to the greenbelt.

left.label: Commit to the coastal mangrove greenbelt.
left.tooltip: A wall of trees stops a wave no concrete can.
left.reveal: You commit land and money to the greenbelt and protect it from encroachment. The shrimp and salt interests resist losing the coast, but a living storm-barrier rises and the fisheries it nurses revive.

right.label: Leave the coast to shrimp and salt.
right.tooltip: The coast earns more in ponds than in trees.
right.reveal: You let the lucrative shrimp and salt keep the coast. The export earnings flow, the greenbelt goes unplanted, and the next cyclone hits a shore stripped of the trees that would have softened it.

speaker: Forest Conservator
prompt: Reserve forest is being carved up and grabbed for resorts, plantations, and estates by the politically powerful, the boundary pillars quietly moved. The conservator can mount evictions and restore the boundaries, confronting connected grabbers, or look the other way.

left.label: Evict the grabbers, restore the forest.
left.tooltip: A reserve forest sold off in pieces is a lung removed.
left.reveal: You evict the encroachers and reset the boundaries, however connected they are. The powerful grabbers rage and litigate, but the reserve forest is clawed back from the resort-builders and planters.

right.label: Look the other way.
right.tooltip: The men moving those pillars are men I rely on.
right.reveal: You leave the encroachments alone. The connected grabbers keep their slices of forest, the boundary pillars stay where they were quietly moved, and the reserve shrinks resort by resort.

speaker: Renewable Energy Adviser
prompt: Land is scarce, but your reservoirs, canals, and factory rooftops could host vast floating and rooftop solar arrays. The adviser wants a hard mandate and incentives to cover them; the utilities and some industries resist the disruption and cost.

left.label: Mandate floating and rooftop solar.
left.tooltip: A nation short of land is rich in roofs and water.
left.reveal: You mandate solar on reservoirs, canals, and rooftops. The utilities and industries grumble at the cost and disruption, but clean power grows without consuming farmland, and imports ease.

right.label: Leave solar deployment to the market.
right.tooltip: A mandate forces cost on the unwilling.
right.reveal: You leave solar to voluntary uptake. The disruption and cost are avoided, and the rooftops and reservoirs stay bare while the country keeps burning imported fuel it need not.

speaker: Mining Bureau Director
prompt: The country's only hard-rock mine, run at a chronic loss under a padded foreign operating contract, supplies stone the construction boom craves. The director can renegotiate the corrupt contract and modernise — angering connected middlemen — or keep importing the difference.

left.label: Renegotiate the contract and modernise.
left.tooltip: A mine that loses money on every stone is a contract, not a mine.
left.reveal: You tear up the padded contract and modernise operations. The connected middlemen who skimmed it howl, but the mine moves toward profit and supplies the construction stone now imported at cost.

right.label: Keep the contract and import the rest.
right.tooltip: The men running that contract are not lightly crossed.
right.reveal: You leave the loss-making contract intact and import the shortfall. The middlemen stay happy and the mine stays a drain, and the construction boom keeps paying to import stone you have under your own ground.

speaker: Fertilizer Supply Director
prompt: The country imports nearly all the rock phosphate and potash its fertilizer depends on, leaving farmers exposed to world price spikes. The director wants costly long-term supply contracts and buffer stocks; the alternative is cheaper spot buying and exposure to the next shock.

left.label: Lock in long-term supply and buffers.
left.tooltip: A harvest hostage to a world price is a famine on a futures chart.
left.reveal: You sign long-term phosphate and potash contracts and build buffer stocks. The cost and commitment are real, but the farmers are shielded from the next world price spike that would have wrecked a season.

right.label: Buy cheap on the spot market.
right.tooltip: Why pay to hedge a price that might fall?
right.reveal: You keep buying fertilizer feedstock cheaply on the spot market. The savings are real in calm years, and the next price spike passes straight through to the farmers and the harvest.

speaker: Energy Access Director
prompt: Cheap, clean-burning improved cookstoves would cut the woodsmoke killing rural women and the deforestation feeding their fires — a modest, proven intervention that nonetheless needs subsidy, distribution, and the patience to change a deep habit.

left.label: Roll out improved cookstoves widely.
left.tooltip: A better stove is a longer life and a standing tree.
left.reveal: You subsidise and distribute clean cookstoves across the villages. The habit is slow to change and the cost real, but women's lungs and the forests both get relief, fire by fire.

right.label: Leave cooking habits alone.
right.tooltip: A stove programme is a small return for a real cost.
right.reveal: You skip the cookstove push. The budget is spared, and rural women keep cooking over smoky open fires, their lungs and the surrounding forests paying the quiet price.

speaker: Power Cell Director
prompt: The old independent power-producer contracts lock in dollar-indexed tariffs far above today's market, draining the utility — and they were signed with firms tied to your own party. The director can force a renegotiation, angering the donors, or honour the ruinous terms.

left.label: Force the IPP contracts down.
left.tooltip: A tariff from a fatter year is a wound in a leaner one.
left.reveal: You force the IPPs to renegotiate toward market rates. The party-linked producers rage and threaten arbitration, but the utility stops bleeding dollars on power priced for a richer decade.

right.label: Honour the costly IPP terms.
right.tooltip: My donors' contracts are not lightly reopened.
right.reveal: You honour the old terms to keep the donor-producers content. They stay loyal and rich, and the utility keeps paying dollar-indexed tariffs that drain it and, through it, every consumer.

speaker: Water Supply Director
prompt: The big cities drink their aquifers dry because surface-water treatment is costly to build. The director wants a major shift to treated river water to save the sinking groundwater; the capital is enormous and the rivers themselves are filthy and need cleaning first.

left.label: Build the surface-water supply system.
left.tooltip: A city drinking its own foundations away is doomed.
left.reveal: You commit to treated surface water and the river-cleaning it requires. The capital is vast and the build long, but the aquifers steady and the cities gain a sustainable source of drink.

right.label: Keep pumping the groundwater.
right.tooltip: Surface water is a budget for the next decade, not this one.
right.reveal: You keep the cities on cheap, fast groundwater. The money is saved now, and the aquifers keep falling and the land keeps sinking toward the day the wells run dry or the ground gives way.

speaker: Livestock Development Director
prompt: The country imports most of its milk powder while smallholder dairy farmers struggle for want of feed, breeds, and a fair market. The director wants a costly push for domestic dairy self-sufficiency; the importers and the cheaper foreign powder resist.

left.label: Drive domestic dairy self-sufficiency.
left.tooltip: A nation that imports its milk has surrendered its breakfast.
left.reveal: You invest in feed, breeds, chilling, and markets for smallholder dairy. The importers fight and the build is slow, but domestic milk grows and a vast cohort of small farmers gains a living.

right.label: Keep importing cheap milk powder.
right.tooltip: Cheap powder feeds children the budget cannot.
right.reveal: You keep relying on imported powder. Milk stays cheap on the shelf, and the smallholder dairy farmers keep struggling against a foreign product the state will not help them beat.

speaker: Water Development Officer
prompt: A vast programme to re-excavate the silted-up ponds and canals would restore irrigation, fish, and flood-buffering across the countryside — labour-intensive, leak-prone to local graft, but transformative for rural water. The officer asks whether to launch it big.

left.label: Launch the big re-excavation programme.
left.tooltip: A cleared canal waters a hundred fields.
left.reveal: You launch the mass re-excavation, creating rural jobs and restoring water bodies. Some funds leak to local graft, but irrigation, fisheries, and flood-buffering revive across the countryside.

right.label: Keep it small and tightly controlled.
right.tooltip: A vast labour scheme is a vast skimming opportunity.
right.reveal: You run only a modest, closely-audited version. The graft is contained, and most silted ponds and canals stay choked, their irrigation and flood-buffering lost to the countryside.

speaker: Agriculture Research Director
prompt: A handful of seed companies are cornering the market with hybrids that cannot be saved and replanted, binding farmers to buy anew each season. The director wants a strong public seed system to preserve farmer-saved varieties; the seed firms lobby hard against it.

left.label: Build a strong public seed system.
left.tooltip: A farmer who cannot save his seed is a tenant of a corporation.
left.reveal: You fund public seed banks and farmer-saved varieties alongside the hybrids. The seed companies lobby furiously, but farmers keep an alternative to buying their seed anew every single season.

right.label: Leave seeds to the market.
right.tooltip: The hybrids yield more; the market knows best.
