left.reveal: The majors move in with capital and speed. Gas flows sooner, but the production-sharing terms send the lion's share of the value out of the country.

right.label: Fund Bapex to develop the blocks itself.
right.tooltip: Gas drawn by our own hands stays in our own pockets.
right.reveal: You back the national company. Development is slower and riskier, but a strike would belong wholly to the nation, building skill that outlasts any contract.

speaker: Irrigation Authority Head
prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. The deep tube-wells that made the parched Barind tract bloom with rice are draining the aquifer faster than the rains refill it. The head warns of a slow-motion desert; the farmers warn that any cut to pumping means a failed harvest now.

left.label: Keep the pumps running at full tilt.
left.tooltip: A failed harvest topples a government faster than a dry decade.
left.reveal: The rice keeps coming and the farmers stay calm. The water table sinks another year toward the day the wells suck only dust.

right.label: Cap pumping, shift to drought-hardy crops.
right.tooltip: A region that mines its own water has a deadline.
right.reveal: You ration the aquifer and push less-thirsty crops. This season's yields dip and the farmers grumble, but the Barind may still have water in a generation.

speaker: Environment Inspector
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. The leather tanneries, relocated to a new estate, still dump untreated chromium effluent straight into the river because the promised treatment plant was never finished. The inspector wants them shut until it works; the exporters threaten to flee the country.

left.label: Let the tanneries keep running.
left.tooltip: Leather earns dollars the river cannot.
left.reveal: The exports continue and the chromium flows. A whole river system and the people who drink from it pay the toll, and the unbuilt treatment plant stays a fiction.

right.label: Shut them until the treatment plant works.
right.tooltip: A river is not a sewer for export earnings.
right.reveal: You close the estate until it cleans up. Exporters rage and some relocate abroad, but the river gets a chance and the rule finally has teeth.

speaker: Ports Development Chairman
prompt: The same fault line, the harder hour, the narrower path. A deep-sea port would transform trade — and three different foreign backers (one Japanese, one Chinese, one Indian) are competing to build it, each deal carrying strategic strings. The chairman needs you to pick a patron, or build it slower on your own dime.

left.label: Take the cheapest foreign deal and its strings.
left.tooltip: A port now is worth a patron's leash.
left.reveal: Construction races ahead on foreign money. The port rises fast, and so does the geopolitical debt — the chosen patron now has a foothold on your coast.

right.label: Build slower, financed at home.
right.tooltip: A harbour bought with strings docks foreign warships.
right.reveal: You forgo the fast foreign money for a slower, self-funded build. The port is years away, but no rival power gains a berth in your strategic waters.

speaker: District Mineral Officer
prompt: It comes back sharpened by every season you survived. In the northeast, illegal stone quarrying is gutting the riverbeds and hill slopes, feeding the construction boom but collapsing banks and killing labourers in pit cave-ins. The officer asks whether to enforce the ban or keep the cheap aggregate flowing.

left.label: Let the quarrying continue.
left.tooltip: The cities are built on this stone.
left.reveal: The aggregate keeps the construction sector cheap and humming. The rivers braid into ruin, the slopes slump, and another pit swallows another crew.

right.label: Enforce the quarrying ban.
right.tooltip: Stone pulled from a living river kills it.
right.reveal: You shut the illegal pits and the riverbeds begin to heal. Construction costs tick up and the local mafias lose a fortune, sending their muscle against your officers.

speaker: Fisheries Director
prompt: A later year of power, the old crisis now a true test of it. The national fish spawns only if you enforce a strict fishing ban during the breeding season — but that means tens of thousands of fishing families with no income for weeks. The director can enforce the ban hard, or look away to spare the hunger.

left.label: Enforce the breeding-season ban hard.
left.tooltip: A fish that cannot spawn feeds no one next year.
left.reveal: Patrols keep the nets out of the water and the stocks rebound spectacularly. The fishing families go hungry for the weeks of the ban, and resent every patrol boat.

right.label: Relax the ban to spare the families.
right.tooltip: A starving fisherman cannot wait for next season.
right.reveal: You ease enforcement and the families eat. The catch this season holds, but the spawning falters and next year's nets come up lighter.

speaker: Renewable Energy Adviser
prompt: The unresolved returns, and this time it will not be deferred. A net-metering scheme would let factories and homes put rooftop solar on the grid and sell back the surplus — cutting imports and emissions. The utilities resist, since every rooftop panel is a customer they lose. The adviser asks whose side you take.

left.label: Side with the utilities, stall net metering.
left.tooltip: A grid undercut by a million rooftops collapses.
left.reveal: You protect the utilities' monopoly and the rooftops stay bare. Fuel imports stay high, and the clean-energy future drifts another few years out of reach.

right.label: Mandate generous net metering.
right.tooltip: Every rooftop is a power plant I did not have to build.
right.reveal: You force the grid open to rooftop solar. The utilities howl about lost revenue, but imports ease, the air clears, and a solar industry takes root.

speaker: Grid Interconnection Officer
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. You can import cheap hydropower across the border through a regional grid — clean, plentiful, and a hedge against fuel shocks. But it deepens dependence on a giant neighbour who could, in a quarrel, simply pull the plug.

left.label: Sign the cross-border power import.
left.tooltip: Cheap clean power outweighs a distant risk.
left.reveal: The hydropower flows in and bills ease. Your grid now leans on a neighbour's goodwill, a switch in a foreign capital that could one day darken your cities.

right.label: Decline; build domestic capacity instead.
right.tooltip: A switch in another's hand is a noose in waiting.
right.reveal: You forgo the cheap import for self-reliance. Power stays costlier and dirtier for now, but no foreign hand rests on your grid's master switch.

speaker: Agriculture Minister
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. The state fertilizer plants devour scarce gas to make cheap urea for farmers — gas the power sector is desperate for. The minister asks whether to keep feeding the fertilizer plants for food security, or divert the gas to keep the lights on.

left.label: Keep gas flowing to fertilizer.
left.tooltip: Cheap urea is cheap rice is a calm country.
left.reveal: The farmers get their subsidised urea and the harvest holds. The power sector starves of the diverted gas, and load-shedding creeps back into the cities.

right.label: Divert the gas to power generation.
right.tooltip: A dark grid stops a country faster than dear fertilizer.
right.reveal: You redirect the gas to the turbines and the lights stay on. Fertilizer prices climb, farmers protest, and the next harvest carries a question mark.

speaker: Power Cell Director
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. The notorious law that lets you award power-plant contracts with no tender and no legal challenge — the source of the ruinous 'capacity charges' paid to idle plants — is up for renewal. The director's friends in the rental business want it extended again.

left.label: Renew the no-tender indemnity law.
left.tooltip: My donors' plants must keep their guarantees.
left.reveal: The law is extended and the capacity charges keep flowing to idle plants. Your rental-baron donors are delighted, and the public keeps paying for power it never receives.

right.label: Let the indemnity law lapse.
right.tooltip: Paying for electricity that never comes is a scandal I will end.
right.reveal: You let the shield expire and open the sector to tenders and lawsuits. The rental barons rage and threaten, but the rip-off capacity charges finally face daylight.

speaker: Environment Inspector
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. Thousands of illegal brick kilns ring the cities, burning coal and the country's fertile topsoil into smog that chokes the capital every winter. The inspector wants them forced onto clean technology or shut; the kiln owners employ a million workers.

left.label: Leave the kilns as they are.
left.tooltip: A million jobs outweigh a season of smog.
left.reveal: The kilns keep firing and the workers keep their wages. The capital's winter air becomes the world's worst, and the cropland loses its skin one kiln at a time.

right.label: Force clean kilns or closure.
right.tooltip: Bricks baked from topsoil and smog cost more than they earn.
right.reveal: You mandate cleaner zigzag technology and shut the worst offenders. Some kilns close and workers are displaced, but the air and the farmland get a reprieve.

speaker: Coast Guard Commander
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. Foreign industrial trawlers are sweeping your newly-won maritime zone bare, often with bribed permits, while artisanal fishers come home empty. The commander can mount costly patrols to drive them out, or keep taking the licence fees and look away.

left.label: Keep the licence fees, look away.
left.tooltip: The fees are certain; the patrols are expensive.
left.reveal: The foreign trawlers keep dragging your waters and the fees fill a ledger. The small fishers' nets come up empty, and the fishery heads toward collapse.

right.label: Patrol hard and expel the trawlers.
right.tooltip: A sea swept bare by foreigners feeds no one here.
right.reveal: You fund the patrols and drive the foreign fleets out. It costs the navy dearly and angers the licence-buyers' governments, but the stocks and the artisanal fishers recover.

speaker: Industries Secretary
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. The country has become a dumping ground for the world's electronic waste, picked apart by hand in toxic backyard yards — including children's. The secretary can crack down and demand a formal recycling industry, or keep the cheap scrap trade flowing.

left.label: Let the informal e-waste trade run.
left.tooltip: Scrap is scrap, and it employs the poor.
left.reveal: The backyard yards keep smelting circuit boards over open fires. The lead and cadmium seep into the children who work them, and the world keeps shipping its poison here.

right.label: Mandate a formal recycling industry.
right.tooltip: I will not let the world poison our children for scrap.
right.reveal: You ban the backyard trade and license proper recyclers. The cheap scrap economy contracts, but the toxic exposure falls and a real industry begins to form.

speaker: Forest Conservator
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. To protect the mangrove forest you can bar the honey-collectors, woodcutters, and fishers whose families have lived off it for centuries — or let them keep harvesting and watch the forest thin. The conservator says the forest cannot bear both protection and people forever.

left.label: Bar the forest communities for conservation.
left.tooltip: A forest emptied of axes survives.
left.reveal: You evict the harvesters and the mangroves recover. Generations of forest families lose their only livelihood, and resentment festers along the forest's edge.

right.label: Let the communities keep harvesting sustainably.
right.tooltip: A forest is kept by those who live in it, not those who fence it.
right.reveal: You license sustainable, limited harvesting and bring the communities into protection. The forest thins more slowly, and its people become its guardians instead of its enemies.

speaker: Jute Ministry Secretary
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. The state jute mills — once the 'golden fibre' that built the economy — bleed losses every year, kept alive for the workers and the heritage. The secretary can shut the rotting mills to stop the bleeding, or pour in another bailout and a modernisation gamble.

left.label: Shut the loss-making jute mills.
left.tooltip: Nostalgia is not a balance sheet.
left.reveal: You close the mills and stem the losses. Tens of thousands of workers and a piece of national identity are cast off, and the jute towns turn against you.

right.label: Bail out and modernise the mills.
right.tooltip: The golden fibre may yet be gold again.
right.reveal: You fund a modernisation push toward jute textiles and bioplastics. It is a costly gamble, but the workers keep their looms and a heritage industry gets one more chance.

speaker: Coal Import Coordinator
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. Your new coal plants need a steady stream of imported coal, and a dedicated deep-water terminal would lock in cheap, reliable supply for decades. It also chains the country to coal long after the world has moved to phase it out — and to financing it.

left.label: Build the dedicated coal terminal.
left.tooltip: My plants are built; they must be fed.
left.reveal: The terminal secures decades of cheap coal for the plants you have. You also build infrastructure the world is racing to abandon, a stranded asset waiting to happen.

right.label: Cancel the terminal, plan for coal's exit.
right.tooltip: A terminal for a dying fuel is a tomb I am paying to dig.
right.reveal: You scrap the terminal and begin planning coal's phase-out. The existing plants run short-fed and costly, but you stop sinking money into a stranded future.

speaker: WASA Managing Director
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. The capital's piped water is so contaminated that millions boil it or buy it by the jar, while the rivers ringing the city are biologically dead. The MD needs a vast, expensive overhaul of treatment and sewerage — or you can keep patching and let people cope.

left.label: Keep patching the old system.
left.tooltip: A full overhaul is a budget I do not have.
left.reveal: You make do with repairs and the taps keep running brown. The dead rivers stay dead, the bottled-water bills crush the poor, and waterborne disease simmers on.

right.label: Fund the full water and sewerage overhaul.
right.tooltip: A capital that cannot drink its own water is failing.
right.reveal: You commit to the costly rebuild of treatment and sewers. The budget groans for years, but a generation may finally drink from the tap without fear.

speaker: Petrobangla Chairman
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. A foreign operator's well has blown out, burning a forest and a gas reserve worth a fortune for weeks. The company offers a token settlement and a denial of liability. The chairman asks whether to fight for full compensation or take the quiet money and move on.

left.label: Take the token settlement quietly.
left.tooltip: A long fight scares off the next investor.
left.reveal: You accept the pittance and sign away the claim. The reserve and the forest are gone for nothing, and every future operator learns that recklessness here is cheap.

right.label: Demand full compensation in court.
right.tooltip: A burned reserve is a debt the burner will pay.
right.reveal: You sue for the true value of the lost gas and the scorched land. The investor climate cools and the case drags, but you set a precedent that negligence here has a price.

speaker: Energy Secretary
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. With gas reserves dwindling, you have frozen new household pipeline connections — but millions on the waiting list, who have paid bribes for years, are furious. The secretary can lift the freeze to win goodwill, or hold it to ration the dying reserve for industry.

left.label: Lift the freeze, connect the households.
left.tooltip: A promised connection denied is a vote lost.
left.reveal: The taps open for the waiting millions and the goodwill flows. The reserve drains faster toward the day the gas simply runs out, for homes and factories alike.

right.label: Hold the freeze, ration gas for industry.
right.tooltip: Gas burned in a stove builds nothing; gas in a factory builds a country.
right.reveal: You keep the freeze and steer the gas to industry and power. The waiting millions seethe, but the reserve stretches further and the factories keep running.

speaker: Bridges Authority Chairman
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. A signature river tunnel has opened, financed by foreign loans, but traffic is a fraction of the rosy forecasts that justified it. The chairman can set punishing tolls to service the debt, or keep tolls low and let the loss sit on the public books.

left.label: Set high tolls to service the debt.
left.tooltip: The users of the tunnel should pay for it.
left.reveal: The tolls bite and the debt is serviced. Hauliers reroute to avoid the cost, traffic thins further, and the showpiece becomes a monument to optimistic forecasts.

right.label: Keep tolls low, absorb the loss.
right.tooltip: A tunnel no one can afford to use is a tomb.
right.reveal: You keep the toll cheap and the traffic builds. The debt sits unpaid on the public ledger, a quiet weight that some future budget will have to carry.

speaker: Transport Electrification Adviser
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. Imported fuel for vehicles drains your dollars by the billion. The adviser proposes aggressive incentives for electric vehicles and a charging network — slashing the oil bill over time, but stranding the gas-station economy and demanding more grid power up front.

left.label: Push hard for the EV transition.
left.tooltip: Every electric mile is a dollar that stays home.
left.reveal: Incentives and chargers roll out and the EVs come. The fuel-import bill begins its long decline, while the grid strains and the petrol-pump economy starts to wither.

right.label: Stick with the existing fuel fleet.
right.tooltip: A grid this fragile cannot charge a nation's cars.
right.reveal: You hold off and keep the petrol economy intact. The fuel-import bill keeps bleeding dollars, but the strained grid is spared and the pump-workers keep their trade.

speaker: Renewable Energy Adviser
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. The coast has strong, steady wind, and a foreign developer offers to build a large wind farm — clean power, but on prime coastal land that fishers and salt-farmers use, and at a guaranteed tariff higher than coal.

left.label: Approve the coastal wind farm.
left.tooltip: Wind is the one fuel no one can embargo.
left.reveal: The turbines rise and clean power flows. The fishers and salt-farmers lose ground and the tariff is steep, but the country gains a fuel free of import and emission.

right.label: Reject it to protect the coastal commons.
right.tooltip: A green plant on stolen land is not clean.
right.reveal: You refuse to displace the coastal livelihoods for the turbines. The clean-power gain is forgone, but the fishers and salt-farmers keep the shore they have always worked.

speaker: Land Reform Commissioner
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. New river islands — fertile silt 'chars' — have surfaced, and they are state land by law. The commissioner can distribute them to the landless families who already farm them, or let the local strongmen who 'manage' the chars formalise their grab.

left.label: Let the strongmen formalise their hold.
left.tooltip: The chars are already governed by those who hold them.
left.reveal: The mastans get their titles and the landless become their tenants. A vast tract of fertile new land entrenches the very feudalism land reform was meant to end.

right.label: Distribute the chars to the landless.
right.tooltip: New land should make new owners, not new landlords.
right.reveal: You title the chars to the families who farm them. The strongmen lose a prize and send their thugs, but thousands of landless households gain ground of their own.

speaker: Wetlands Director
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. The vast northeastern haor wetlands — a single boro rice crop, a fishery, a bird sanctuary — are being diked and converted to year-round farming, which raises yields but destroys the flood-pulse the whole ecosystem depends on. The director begs you to choose.

left.label: Allow the diking for higher yields.
left.tooltip: More crops from the same water is progress.
left.reveal: The dikes go up and the rice doubles for a while. The fishery crashes, the birds vanish, and one bad flash-flood now drowns a crop the wetland used to absorb.

right.label: Protect the natural flood-pulse.
right.tooltip: A wetland drained is a famine deferred.
right.reveal: You halt the conversion and protect the flood-pulse. The single-crop yields stay modest, but the fishery, the birds, and the wetland's flood defence all survive.

speaker: Tourism Board Chair
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. The country's only coral island is being loved to death — hotels on the dunes, day-trippers trampling the reef, plastic everywhere. The chair can cap visitors and tear down illegal resorts to save the reef, or keep the tourist revenue flowing.

left.label: Keep the tourism flowing.
left.tooltip: The island earns its keep in tourist taka.
left.reveal: The hotels stay and the boats keep coming. The reef bleaches and breaks under the crowds, and the one coral ecosystem in the country slides toward death.

right.label: Cap visitors, demolish illegal resorts.
right.tooltip: A dead reef draws no one.
right.reveal: You ration access and raze the illegal hotels. The local tourist economy takes a blow and the resort-owners sue, but the reef gets a fighting chance to recover.

speaker: Environment Secretary
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. Single-use plastic chokes every drain, river, and field in the country — and clogs the very canals whose blockage floods the capital. The secretary proposes a hard ban and a jute-bag mandate. The plastics industry, a big employer and donor, will fight to the death.

left.label: Hold off on the plastics ban.
left.tooltip: A ban that kills an industry kills jobs and donors.
left.reveal: You spare the plastics industry and the bags keep flowing. The drains clog, the floods worsen, and the microplastics work their way into the fish and the people.

right.label: Enforce the ban, mandate jute bags.
right.tooltip: A drain choked with plastic drowns a city.
right.reveal: You ban the worst single-use plastics and revive the jute-bag trade. The plastics lobby rages and some jobs vanish, but the drains clear and a jute revival stirs.

speaker: Geological Survey Director
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. The coastal beaches hold valuable heavy-mineral sands — zircon, titanium, rare earths — that a foreign miner wants to extract. It would earn real money, but strip-mining the beaches would gut the storm-buffering dunes and the turtle nesting grounds.

left.label: Approve the beach mineral mining.
left.tooltip: Rare earths in the sand are riches wasted unmined.
left.reveal: The miners move in and the export earnings begin. The dunes that blunt the cyclones are carried away by the truckload, and the coast loses its natural armour.

right.label: Keep the beaches and dunes intact.
right.tooltip: Dunes that stop a cyclone are worth more than zircon.
right.reveal: You refuse the concession. The mineral wealth stays buried, but the dunes keep standing between the storms and the people behind them.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. The coastal embankments that hold back the tides are decades old and failing; one big surge could drown a district. The chief needs a massive repair programme — or you can keep doing emergency patch-jobs after each breach, cheaper now and catastrophic later.

left.label: Keep patching breaches as they come.
left.tooltip: A full rebuild is a budget I will spend on the next disaster.
left.reveal: You fund only the emergency repairs. Each cyclone breaches the weak embankments anew, salting fields and drowning homes the rebuild would have saved.

right.label: Fund the full embankment rebuild.
right.tooltip: An embankment is cheap next to the district it saves.
right.reveal: You commit to rebuilding the polders properly. The cost is huge and slow to show, but a coastal district gains a real wall against the rising, angrier sea.

speaker: Industries Secretary
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. The construction boom runs on cement made from imported clinker and limestone, draining dollars. A domestic limestone quarry in the hills could cut the import bill — at the cost of blasting a forested range the indigenous communities hold sacred.

left.label: Open the domestic limestone quarry.
left.tooltip: A boom this big cannot run on imports forever.
left.reveal: The quarry opens and the cement-import bill eases. The sacred range is blasted apart, the indigenous communities are displaced, and another grievance hardens in the hills.

right.label: Keep importing, spare the sacred range.
right.tooltip: Some hills are worth more standing than crushed.
right.reveal: You leave the range intact and keep paying for imports. The dollar drain continues, but the forest and the communities who revere it are spared the dynamite.

speaker: Fisheries Director
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. The booming farmed-fish and shrimp sector dopes its ponds with banned antibiotics, and Europe is threatening to reject the exports over residue limits. The director can crack down hard — costly and unpopular with farmers — or keep certifying and hope the testing misses it.

left.label: Keep certifying, hope the tests miss it.
left.tooltip: A crackdown now empties a thousand ponds.
left.reveal: You keep the exports flowing on a wink. One bad batch detected abroad triggers a blanket ban, and a whole export sector collapses overnight on a gamble.

right.label: Crack down on the banned antibiotics.
right.tooltip: An export caught dirty loses the whole market.
right.reveal: You enforce the residue limits hard. The farmers howl at the cost and the disease losses, but the exports clear European labs and the market stays open.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. The country's rivers — its cheapest highways — are silting up, stranding the cargo boats that move grain and goods. A vast dredging programme would reopen them, but the dredged spoil has to go somewhere, and the cheapest dumping ruins floodplains and fisheries.

left.label: Dredge fast and dump spoil cheaply.
left.tooltip: Open rivers move a nation; tidy disposal can wait.
left.reveal: The channels reopen and the cargo flows again. The carelessly dumped spoil buries floodplains and chokes fisheries, trading one waterway's life for another's.

right.label: Dredge carefully with managed spoil.
right.tooltip: A river reopened by ruining another is no gain.
right.reveal: You dredge with proper spoil management and floodplain protection. It is slower and dearer, but the rivers reopen without killing the wetlands beside them.

speaker: Petrobangla Chairman
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. With the maritime boundary settled, you can finally open the deep offshore blocks to international bidding. The majors want generous, low-royalty terms to take the deep-water risk. The chairman asks how hard to bargain — gas in hand, or the nation's full share.

left.label: Offer generous terms to attract the majors.
left.tooltip: Gas undiscovered earns nothing; bring the rigs.
left.reveal: The sweet terms draw the majors and the drilling begins. Gas may flow within years, but the low royalties mean the nation pockets a thin slice of its own seabed.

right.label: Bargain hard for the nation's full share.
right.tooltip: Our seabed, our terms.
right.reveal: You hold out for higher royalties and faster work commitments. Some bidders walk and exploration slows, but any gas found will pay the nation what it is worth.

speaker: Commerce Secretary
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. A glut has crashed the price of locally-farmed salt, ruining the coastal salt-farmers, while industry lobbies to import cheap foreign salt for its factories. The secretary asks whether to protect the farmers with an import ban or let the cheap salt in.

left.label: Allow the cheap salt imports.
left.tooltip: Industry runs on the cheapest input.
left.reveal: The factories get their cheap salt and the lobby is pleased. The coastal salt-farmers, undercut to ruin, abandon the pans, and a coastal livelihood dies out.

right.label: Ban imports to protect the salt-farmers.
right.tooltip: A coast of ruined salt-pans is a coast of empty hands.
right.reveal: You shut out the foreign salt and prop up the local price. Industry grumbles at the cost, but the salt-farmers keep their pans and their precarious living.

speaker: Forest Conservator
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. To hit your 'green coverage' targets cheaply, the department wants to blanket cleared hills with fast-growing acacia and eucalyptus monoculture — green on paper, but a desert for wildlife that sucks the water table dry. The alternative is slower native-species restoration.

left.label: Plant the fast monoculture for green targets.
left.tooltip: A number on a chart is a number won.
left.reveal: The hills go green with acacia and the coverage statistics soar. Beneath the uniform canopy nothing lives, and the water table drops under the thirsty exotics.

right.label: Restore slow native forest instead.
right.tooltip: A plantation is not a forest.
right.reveal: You fund mixed native restoration. The 'green coverage' rises slowly and the targets slip, but a real forest — water, wildlife, and all — begins to return.

speaker: Petrobangla Chairman
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. Exploration has struck a major new onshore gas field — a genuine windfall. The chairman asks how to use it: burn it fast and cheap to win an easy few years of plenty, or husband it as a long-term reserve and feedstock for industry.

left.label: Burn it fast for a few easy years.
left.tooltip: Plenty now buys loyalty now.
left.reveal: You open the taps and the country enjoys cheap, abundant gas. The field empties in a handful of years, and the windfall is gone before it built anything lasting.

right.label: Husband it as a long-term reserve.
right.tooltip: A field burned in five years is five years of folly.
right.reveal: You meter the new field for industry and the long haul. The public sees no instant bonanza, but the reserve underwrites a decade of factories and fertiliser.

speaker: Power Development Chairman
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. The coal plant beside the mangrove forest is running, and its backers now want a second, larger unit on the same site. The chairman calls it cheap megawatts on proven ground; the scientists warn it doubles the threat to the forest the country shelters behind.

left.label: Approve the second coal unit.
left.tooltip: Proven ground builds the cheapest megawatts.
left.reveal: The expansion is greenlit and the capacity doubles. So does the ash, the warm effluent, and the shipping traffic gnawing at the forest's edge.

right.label: Cap the site, build clean capacity elsewhere.
right.tooltip: I will not double a bet against the forest.
right.reveal: You forbid the second unit and steer new capacity to cleaner sites. The cheap megawatts are forgone, but the mangrove shield is not made to carry twice the load.

speaker: Hill Resources Officer
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. A survey has found valuable minerals under indigenous land in the hill tracts. The communities, citing the peace accord, demand consent and a share; the mining lobby says national resources belong to the nation, accord or not. The officer awaits your call.

left.label: Mine without indigenous consent.
left.tooltip: The nation's minerals are the nation's, not a clan's.
left.reveal: You authorise extraction over the communities' objections. The minerals flow and so does the fury; the peace accord, already brittle, cracks a little wider.

right.label: Require consent and a community share.
right.tooltip: Wealth dug from a people's land owes that people.
right.reveal: You make extraction conditional on consent and royalties to the communities. The lobby fumes at the precedent, but the hills, for once, share in their own ground's wealth.

speaker: Irrigation Authority Head
prompt: Late in the reign, the old question demands a heavier answer. Free and unmetered irrigation power has farmers pumping groundwater with abandon. The head proposes prepaid meters on the irrigation pumps to curb the waste and the subsidy bleed — but a metered pump in a bad season is a farmer's ruin.

left.label: Meter the irrigation pumps.
left.tooltip: Water and power given free are water and power wasted.
left.reveal: The meters go on and the reckless pumping eases, sparing the aquifer and the subsidy. In the dry years, though, a metered pump becomes one more cost a failing farmer cannot meet.

right.label: Keep irrigation free and unmetered.
right.tooltip: A meter on a farmer's pump is a meter on his harvest.
right.reveal: You leave the pumps free and the farmers easy. The aquifer keeps falling and the subsidy keeps bleeding, but no farmer loses a crop to a card with no credit on it.

speaker: Labour Inspector
prompt: It returns once more — and this round may be the one remembered. The ship-breaking beaches that feed the country's steel are death-traps — no safety gear, asbestos, men cut in half by falling plate, child labour at the torches. The inspector demands enforced standards; the yard owners say the margins cannot bear them.

left.label: Leave the yards to their own ways.
left.tooltip: Cheap steel needs cheap hands.
left.reveal: The yards keep running on blood and asbestos. The steel stays cheap, the casualty list grows, and the next exposé brands the beaches the world's most lethal workplace.

right.label: Enforce hard safety standards.
right.tooltip: Steel bought with a man a week is too dear.
right.reveal: You force protective gear, asbestos controls, and an end to child labour. Margins thin and some yards close, but men stop dying by the dozen on the tide line.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: The reckoning circles back, grown teeth in the waiting. Engineers propose a colossal canal to divert water from the wet south to the drought-prone north — a nation-reshaping project. The chief calls it a permanent answer to the north's thirst; ecologists warn it could wreck the river systems at both ends.

left.label: Greenlight the grand canal.
left.tooltip: A nation that moves its own water masters its fate.
left.reveal: The mega-project launches to fanfare and a flood of contracts. The drought-prone north dreams of water, while ecologists count the rivers the canal will starve and drown.

right.label: Shelve it for smaller, local schemes.
right.tooltip: A canal that breaks two rivers to fill one is folly.
right.reveal: You set aside the grand scheme for modest local water projects. The headline-grabbing fix is gone, but the rivers keep their flow and the money spreads wider.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: Another term, the same antagonist, the stakes raised again. You pressed ahead, and the canal's upstream intake has dried the wetlands and fisheries of the donor basin, igniting protests there even as the north celebrates its new water. The donor districts demand the intake be throttled back.

left.label: Keep the intake at full flow for the north.
left.tooltip: The north's thirst was the whole point.
left.reveal: You hold the flow and the north blooms. The donor basin's fisheries collapse and its people, betrayed, turn the canal into a symbol of one region drained for another.

right.label: Throttle the intake to spare the donor basin.
right.tooltip: I will not drown one region to water another.
right.reveal: You cut the diversion back to a sustainable flow. The north's gains shrink and its boosters grumble, but the donor basin's wetlands and fishers are pulled back from ruin.

speaker: Water Development Chief
prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. With the canal half-built and half-cursed, the contractors — politically connected — demand the full budget regardless of the scaled-back flow, threatening to abandon the works and sue. You can pay them off or face a stalled, litigated ruin in the landscape.

left.label: Pay the contractors in full.
left.tooltip: A half-dug canal is worse than none.
left.reveal: You pay out to keep the works moving. The connected contractors pocket the windfall, the budget bleeds, and the project limps on under a cloud of inflated bills.

right.label: Hold them to the scaled-back contract.
right.tooltip: I will not be extorted by my own diggers.
right.reveal: You refuse the padded claim and renegotiate to the reduced scope. The contractors threaten and litigate, but the public is spared paying full price for half a canal.

speaker: Grid Operations Chief
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. The national grid is so brittle that a single fault can — and once did — black out the entire country at once. The chief wants a costly redesign with regional islanding and redundancy; the money is wanted elsewhere until the next nationwide blackout.

left.label: Fund the grid-resilience redesign.
left.tooltip: A grid that fails all at once fails the whole nation at once.
left.reveal: You fund islanding, redundancy, and modern controls. The cost is heavy and the benefit invisible between failures, but a single fault can no longer plunge the entire country into darkness.

right.label: Patch the grid and hope.
right.tooltip: A full redesign is a budget for a disaster that may not come.
right.reveal: You make do with patches. The money goes elsewhere, the brittle grid stays brittle, and the country waits, one bad fault away from another total nationwide blackout.

speaker: Rural Electrification Chief
prompt: The same fault line, the harder hour, the narrower path. Remote villages can be reached either by costly grid extension or by the off-grid solar home systems the country pioneered. The chief asks whether to push solar to the last villages now, or wait years to bring them the grid that powers heavier loads.

left.label: Push off-grid solar to the last villages.
left.tooltip: Light tonight beats a grid in a decade.
left.reveal: You blanket the remote villages with solar home systems. Millions get clean light and phone-charging now, though the small panels cannot run the mills and pumps a grid eventually would.

right.label: Wait and extend the full grid.
right.tooltip: A village deserves real power, not a panel.
right.reveal: You hold out to bring the villages the full grid. They wait years longer in darkness, but when the lines arrive they carry the heavy loads that solar panels never could.

speaker: Power Transition Adviser
prompt: It comes back sharpened by every season you survived. Pressure mounts to retire the ageing, dirty coal plants ahead of schedule. Doing so cleans the air and the country's image, but strands billions in assets and throws thousands of plant and mine workers out of work in towns built around the coal.

left.label: Retire the coal plants with a just transition.
left.tooltip: A dying industry should be eased to rest, not abandoned.
left.reveal: You retire the plants early and fund retraining and new industry for the coal towns. The stranded-asset cost is steep, but the air clears, the image brightens, and the workers are not simply discarded.

right.label: Keep the coal plants running to term.
right.tooltip: I will not strand billions and a workforce for an early exit.
right.reveal: You keep the coal plants running to their full life. The assets and jobs are preserved, the towns stay employed, and the dirty smoke and the climate criticism keep mounting year on year.

speaker: Energy Security Director
prompt: A later year of power, the old crisis now a true test of it. The country holds only days of fuel stock, leaving it dangerously exposed to any supply shock or blockade. The director wants a costly strategic petroleum reserve and bigger storage depots; the capital is large and the threat feels abstract in calm times.

left.label: Build a strategic fuel reserve.
left.tooltip: A nation with days of fuel is a nation days from paralysis.
left.reveal: You build the strategic reserve and expand storage. The capital outlay is large, but the next supply shock or blockade meets a buffer instead of an instant fuel crisis.

right.label: Run on thin fuel stocks.
right.tooltip: A reserve is a fortune frozen against a maybe.
right.reveal: You keep stocks thin to save the capital. The money stays free for other needs, and the country stays days away from paralysis should any supply shock or blockade come.

speaker: Energy Access Director
prompt: The unresolved returns, and this time it will not be deferred. Most rural households still cook on smoky biomass, ruining women's lungs and the forests. A push to make bottled LPG cheap and available would change that — through subsidy and a safe distribution network, or you can leave the woodsmoke as it is.

left.label: Subsidise rural LPG access.
left.tooltip: A woman's lungs are worth more than the price of a cylinder.
left.reveal: You subsidise LPG and build safe distribution. Woodsmoke recedes from kitchens, women's health improves and the forests get a reprieve, and the subsidy and cylinder-safety burden fall on the budget.

right.label: Leave rural cooking to biomass.
right.tooltip: An LPG subsidy is a cost the budget cannot carry.
right.reveal: You leave rural kitchens to firewood and dung. The budget is spared, and women keep breathing woodsmoke into ruined lungs while the forests keep falling for fuel.

speaker: Energy Diplomacy Director
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. A regional gas pipeline could bring steady, cheaper gas across borders for decades — energy security of a kind — but it threads through and depends on neighbours' goodwill, and chains your grid to a fuel and a transit route others control.

left.label: Commit to the cross-border gas pipeline.
left.tooltip: Steady gas for decades is worth a careful diplomacy.
left.reveal: You commit to the pipeline and cheaper gas begins to flow. Your energy security improves and your dependence deepens, your grid now hostage to a transit route and a neighbour's goodwill.

right.label: Invest in domestic supply instead.
right.tooltip: A pipeline through a neighbour is a valve in his hand.
right.reveal: You forgo the pipeline for domestic exploration and LNG. The gas is costlier and slower, but no foreign hand rests on the valve that feeds your grid.

speaker: Atomic Energy Chairman
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. The new nuclear plant will soon produce spent fuel that must be stored safely for generations, and no permanent solution exists. The chairman can fund a proper long-term storage and safety regime now, or rely on the foreign builder's vague take-back promise and hope.

left.label: Fund proper long-term waste storage.
left.tooltip: A poison that lasts ten thousand years deserves a plan.
left.reveal: You invest in genuine spent-fuel storage and safety. The cost is large and the benefit unseen for decades, but you do not leave a radioactive legacy to be improvised by your grandchildren.

right.label: Rely on the builder's take-back promise.
right.tooltip: The Russians said they would handle it; let them.
right.reveal: You lean on the foreign builder's vague assurances and defer the problem. The money is saved now, and a growing pile of spent fuel waits on a promise no contract firmly guarantees.

speaker: Power Regulator
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. Big factories run their own gas-fired captive power plants, abandoning the grid because it is unreliable — which starves the grid of its best customers and worsens it for everyone else. The regulator can force them back onto the grid, or let industry self-supply.

left.label: Force big industry back onto the grid.
left.tooltip: A grid the strong abandon collapses on the weak.
left.reveal: You compel the factories to draw from the grid and surrender their gas. The grid's finances and reliability improve for all, and the industrialists rage at losing their dependable captive power.

right.label: Let industry keep captive power.
right.tooltip: A factory that cannot trust the grid will make its own.
right.reveal: You let the factories self-supply. Industry keeps its reliable power, and the grid, abandoned by its best customers, grows weaker and dearer for the households and small firms left on it.

speaker: Agriculture Research Director
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. A genetically-modified pest-resistant crop could cut pesticide use and lift yields, but activists warn of seed-company dependence and unknown risks, and the public is uneasy. The director asks whether to approve and promote it or hold back.

left.label: Approve and promote the GM crop.
left.tooltip: A crop that needs no poison feeds more for less.
left.reveal: You approve the GM variety and yields rise as pesticide use falls. The activists protest seed dependence and unknown risks, and farmers gain a hardier crop and a tie to the seed company that owns it.

right.label: Hold back on the GM crop.
right.tooltip: I will not bet the food supply on an untested gene.
right.reveal: You decline to approve it, citing precaution. The pesticide-heavy status quo persists and yields stay flat, but the food supply is not staked on a technology the public distrusts and a seed firm controls.

speaker: Coastal Afforestation Chief
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. A planted mangrove greenbelt along the coast would blunt cyclones and storm surges and nurse fisheries — but the land is coveted for shrimp farms and salt pans, and the planting is slow and easily encroached. The chief asks whether to commit to the greenbelt.

left.label: Commit to the coastal mangrove greenbelt.
left.tooltip: A wall of trees stops a wave no concrete can.
left.reveal: You commit land and money to the greenbelt and protect it from encroachment. The shrimp and salt interests resist losing the coast, but a living storm-barrier rises and the fisheries it nurses revive.

right.label: Leave the coast to shrimp and salt.
right.tooltip: The coast earns more in ponds than in trees.
right.reveal: You let the lucrative shrimp and salt keep the coast. The export earnings flow, the greenbelt goes unplanted, and the next cyclone hits a shore stripped of the trees that would have softened it.

speaker: Forest Conservator
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. Reserve forest is being carved up and grabbed for resorts, plantations, and estates by the politically powerful, the boundary pillars quietly moved. The conservator can mount evictions and restore the boundaries, confronting connected grabbers, or look the other way.

left.label: Evict the grabbers, restore the forest.
left.tooltip: A reserve forest sold off in pieces is a lung removed.
left.reveal: You evict the encroachers and reset the boundaries, however connected they are. The powerful grabbers rage and litigate, but the reserve forest is clawed back from the resort-builders and planters.

right.label: Look the other way.
right.tooltip: The men moving those pillars are men I rely on.
right.reveal: You leave the encroachments alone. The connected grabbers keep their slices of forest, the boundary pillars stay where they were quietly moved, and the reserve shrinks resort by resort.

speaker: Renewable Energy Adviser
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. Land is scarce, but your reservoirs, canals, and factory rooftops could host vast floating and rooftop solar arrays. The adviser wants a hard mandate and incentives to cover them; the utilities and some industries resist the disruption and cost.

left.label: Mandate floating and rooftop solar.
left.tooltip: A nation short of land is rich in roofs and water.
left.reveal: You mandate solar on reservoirs, canals, and rooftops. The utilities and industries grumble at the cost and disruption, but clean power grows without consuming farmland, and imports ease.

right.label: Leave solar deployment to the market.
right.tooltip: A mandate forces cost on the unwilling.
right.reveal: You leave solar to voluntary uptake. The disruption and cost are avoided, and the rooftops and reservoirs stay bare while the country keeps burning imported fuel it need not.

speaker: Mining Bureau Director
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. The country's only hard-rock mine, run at a chronic loss under a padded foreign operating contract, supplies stone the construction boom craves. The director can renegotiate the corrupt contract and modernise — angering connected middlemen — or keep importing the difference.

left.label: Renegotiate the contract and modernise.
left.tooltip: A mine that loses money on every stone is a contract, not a mine.
left.reveal: You tear up the padded contract and modernise operations. The connected middlemen who skimmed it howl, but the mine moves toward profit and supplies the construction stone now imported at cost.

right.label: Keep the contract and import the rest.
right.tooltip: The men running that contract are not lightly crossed.
right.reveal: You leave the loss-making contract intact and import the shortfall. The middlemen stay happy and the mine stays a drain, and the construction boom keeps paying to import stone you have under your own ground.

speaker: Fertilizer Supply Director
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. The country imports nearly all the rock phosphate and potash its fertilizer depends on, leaving farmers exposed to world price spikes. The director wants costly long-term supply contracts and buffer stocks; the alternative is cheaper spot buying and exposure to the next shock.

left.label: Lock in long-term supply and buffers.
left.tooltip: A harvest hostage to a world price is a famine on a futures chart.
left.reveal: You sign long-term phosphate and potash contracts and build buffer stocks. The cost and commitment are real, but the farmers are shielded from the next world price spike that would have wrecked a season.

right.label: Buy cheap on the spot market.
right.tooltip: Why pay to hedge a price that might fall?
right.reveal: You keep buying fertilizer feedstock cheaply on the spot market. The savings are real in calm years, and the next price spike passes straight through to the farmers and the harvest.

speaker: Energy Access Director
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. Cheap, clean-burning improved cookstoves would cut the woodsmoke killing rural women and the deforestation feeding their fires — a modest, proven intervention that nonetheless needs subsidy, distribution, and the patience to change a deep habit.

left.label: Roll out improved cookstoves widely.
left.tooltip: A better stove is a longer life and a standing tree.
left.reveal: You subsidise and distribute clean cookstoves across the villages. The habit is slow to change and the cost real, but women's lungs and the forests both get relief, fire by fire.

right.label: Leave cooking habits alone.
right.tooltip: A stove programme is a small return for a real cost.
right.reveal: You skip the cookstove push. The budget is spared, and rural women keep cooking over smoky open fires, their lungs and the surrounding forests paying the quiet price.

speaker: Power Cell Director
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. The old independent power-producer contracts lock in dollar-indexed tariffs far above today's market, draining the utility — and they were signed with firms tied to your own party. The director can force a renegotiation, angering the donors, or honour the ruinous terms.

left.label: Force the IPP contracts down.
left.tooltip: A tariff from a fatter year is a wound in a leaner one.
left.reveal: You force the IPPs to renegotiate toward market rates. The party-linked producers rage and threaten arbitration, but the utility stops bleeding dollars on power priced for a richer decade.

right.label: Honour the costly IPP terms.
right.tooltip: My donors' contracts are not lightly reopened.
right.reveal: You honour the old terms to keep the donor-producers content. They stay loyal and rich, and the utility keeps paying dollar-indexed tariffs that drain it and, through it, every consumer.

speaker: Water Supply Director
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. The big cities drink their aquifers dry because surface-water treatment is costly to build. The director wants a major shift to treated river water to save the sinking groundwater; the capital is enormous and the rivers themselves are filthy and need cleaning first.

left.label: Build the surface-water supply system.
left.tooltip: A city drinking its own foundations away is doomed.
left.reveal: You commit to treated surface water and the river-cleaning it requires. The capital is vast and the build long, but the aquifers steady and the cities gain a sustainable source of drink.

right.label: Keep pumping the groundwater.
right.tooltip: Surface water is a budget for the next decade, not this one.
right.reveal: You keep the cities on cheap, fast groundwater. The money is saved now, and the aquifers keep falling and the land keeps sinking toward the day the wells run dry or the ground gives way.

speaker: Livestock Development Director
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. The country imports most of its milk powder while smallholder dairy farmers struggle for want of feed, breeds, and a fair market. The director wants a costly push for domestic dairy self-sufficiency; the importers and the cheaper foreign powder resist.

left.label: Drive domestic dairy self-sufficiency.
left.tooltip: A nation that imports its milk has surrendered its breakfast.
left.reveal: You invest in feed, breeds, chilling, and markets for smallholder dairy. The importers fight and the build is slow, but domestic milk grows and a vast cohort of small farmers gains a living.

right.label: Keep importing cheap milk powder.
right.tooltip: Cheap powder feeds children the budget cannot.
right.reveal: You keep relying on imported powder. Milk stays cheap on the shelf, and the smallholder dairy farmers keep struggling against a foreign product the state will not help them beat.

speaker: Water Development Officer
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. A vast programme to re-excavate the silted-up ponds and canals would restore irrigation, fish, and flood-buffering across the countryside — labour-intensive, leak-prone to local graft, but transformative for rural water. The officer asks whether to launch it big.

left.label: Launch the big re-excavation programme.
left.tooltip: A cleared canal waters a hundred fields.
left.reveal: You launch the mass re-excavation, creating rural jobs and restoring water bodies. Some funds leak to local graft, but irrigation, fisheries, and flood-buffering revive across the countryside.

right.label: Keep it small and tightly controlled.
right.tooltip: A vast labour scheme is a vast skimming opportunity.
right.reveal: You run only a modest, closely-audited version. The graft is contained, and most silted ponds and canals stay choked, their irrigation and flood-buffering lost to the countryside.

speaker: Agriculture Research Director
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. A handful of seed companies are cornering the market with hybrids that cannot be saved and replanted, binding farmers to buy anew each season. The director wants a strong public seed system to preserve farmer-saved varieties; the seed firms lobby hard against it.

left.label: Build a strong public seed system.
left.tooltip: A farmer who cannot save his seed is a tenant of a corporation.
left.reveal: You fund public seed banks and farmer-saved varieties alongside the hybrids. The seed companies lobby furiously, but farmers keep an alternative to buying their seed anew every single season.

right.label: Leave seeds to the market.
right.tooltip: The hybrids yield more; the market knows best.
right.reveal: You leave the seed market to the companies. Yields from the hybrids are high, and the farmers grow ever more dependent on buying patented seed each season from a shrinking handful of firms.

speaker: Petroleum Refining Director
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. The country's lone, ancient oil refinery meets a fraction of fuel demand, so most fuel is imported already-refined at a premium. The director wants a costly second refinery for self-reliance; the alternative is to keep importing refined product and skip the capital.

left.label: Build the second refinery.
left.tooltip: A nation that cannot refine its own crude pays twice.
left.reveal: You commit to a modern second refinery. The capital is enormous and the build long, but the country gains the capacity to refine its own and stops paying the premium on imported product.

right.label: Keep importing refined fuel.
right.tooltip: A refinery is a fortune for a margin I can simply pay.
right.reveal: You keep importing refined fuel and skip the capital. The money stays free for other needs, and the country stays dependent on foreign refineries and the premium they charge.

speaker: Water Resources Officer
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. As aquifers fall, a programme of managed recharge — capturing monsoon rain to refill the groundwater — could slow the decline. The officer wants mandatory rainwater harvesting in cities and recharge structures in the countryside; it is cheap per site but vast in scale and easy to neglect.

left.label: Mandate recharge and rainwater harvesting.
left.tooltip: Water taken from the ground must be paid back to it.
left.reveal: You mandate harvesting and recharge at scale. Enforcement is a grind across countless sites, but the monsoon's water starts refilling the aquifers instead of running wasted to the sea.

right.label: Leave water use to draw down freely.
right.tooltip: A mandate on every roof and field is unenforceable.
right.reveal: You skip the recharge mandate as impractical. The administrative burden is avoided, and the aquifers keep falling as the monsoon runs off unwasted-into-the-sea and the wells sink deeper.

speaker: Agriculture Policy Director
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. The obsession with thirsty rice drains the aquifers and leaves diets and incomes undiversified. The director wants incentives to shift toward less-thirsty, higher-value crops — pulses, oilseeds, vegetables — against the grain of farmer habit and rice-centric food policy.

left.label: Incentivise crop diversification.
left.tooltip: A country that grows only rice mines its own water.
left.reveal: You incentivise the shift to less-thirsty, higher-value crops. Farmer habit and the rice-first policy resist, but diets diversify, incomes rise, and the aquifer-draining rice monoculture loosens its grip.

right.label: Keep the rice-first policy.
right.tooltip: Rice is food security; do not gamble the staple.
right.reveal: You keep prioritising rice. Food security feels assured and farmers stay on familiar ground, and the thirsty monoculture keeps draining the aquifers and narrowing the nation's diet and incomes.

speaker: Marine Conservation Officer
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. The coast's sea-turtle nesting beaches are being lost to lights, nets, and development. A protected hatchery-and-beach programme would save them and draw eco-tourism, at the cost of restricting the fishing and building that the coastal economy leans on.

left.label: Protect the nesting beaches and hatcheries.
left.tooltip: A coast that loses its turtles loses a wonder and a draw.
left.reveal: You ring off nesting beaches, fund hatcheries, and curb the lights and nets. The coastal fishers and developers chafe at the limits, but the turtles return and eco-tourism follows.

right.label: Leave the coast to fishing and building.
right.tooltip: A turtle is a poor return on prime coast.
right.reveal: You leave the beaches to development and nets. The coastal economy keeps its ground, and the turtle populations dwindle toward local extinction under the lights and the trawls.

speaker: Grid Modernisation Director
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. Solar and wind are useless after dark and in calm without storage, and the grid cannot absorb them. The director wants a costly grid-scale battery programme to make renewables dispatchable; the alternative is to keep leaning on fossil plants for reliable power.

left.label: Invest in grid-scale battery storage.
left.tooltip: Sunshine saved for the evening is sunshine that counts.
left.reveal: You fund battery storage and the grid can finally absorb and time-shift renewables. The capital is steep, but solar and wind become reliable power instead of daytime novelties, and fossil dependence eases.

right.label: Keep leaning on fossil baseload.
right.tooltip: Batteries are a fortune for power I already have.
right.reveal: You skip the storage and keep the fossil plants as backbone. The money is saved, and the renewables you build stay a fair-weather supplement the grid cannot truly rely on.

speaker: Gas Distribution Chief
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. A fifth of the piped gas vanishes through illegal connections, tampered meters, and bribed staff, much of it serving captive industrial and elite users. The chief can mount a hard anti-theft drive that hits connected beneficiaries, or keep absorbing the loss.

left.label: Crack down on the gas theft.
left.tooltip: Gas stolen by the connected is paid for by the metered.
left.reveal: You hunt the illegal connections and tampered meters and purge the complicit staff. The connected thieves and their patrons rage, but the system loss falls and the honest stop subsidising the powerful.

right.label: Absorb the system loss.
right.tooltip: A theft drive against the connected is a war I need not start.
right.reveal: You leave the gas theft alone. The connected keep their illegal taps and tampered meters, and the vanishing fifth of the gas is quietly billed, through tariffs, to those who actually pay.

speaker: Forest Department Chief
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. A social-forestry scheme would let poor villagers grow and harvest trees on degraded state land — income for them, green cover for the country — but done carelessly it becomes a backdoor to clear-felling and grabbing the very forest it should grow. The chief asks how to design it.

left.label: Run a genuine community social-forestry scheme.
left.tooltip: A forest the poor profit from is a forest the poor defend.
left.reveal: You design a real benefit-sharing scheme on degraded land with safeguards against grabbing. The poor gain income and the country green cover, and the forest gains guardians instead of poachers.

right.label: Let the scheme run loose.
right.tooltip: Trees planted are trees planted, however it is done.
right.reveal: You roll it out without tight safeguards. The headline planting numbers rise, and the scheme becomes a backdoor for clear-felling and grabbing the forest under cover of growing it.

speaker: Renewable Research Director
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. The strong tidal range of the estuaries could, in theory, drive tidal power — a clean, novel source. A pilot would be costly and unproven, a gamble on innovation; the safe path is to stick with proven solar and wind and let others pioneer the tides.

left.label: Fund the tidal-energy pilot.
left.tooltip: A nation of rivers should learn to harvest its tides.
left.reveal: You fund the tidal pilot. It is a gamble on unproven technology, and if it works the estuaries gain a clean, novel power source the country could pioneer for the region.

right.label: Stick with proven solar and wind.
right.tooltip: Let richer nations gamble on the tides.
right.reveal: You skip the tidal gamble for proven renewables. The capital is spared the risk, and the chance to pioneer a novel clean source suited to your estuaries passes to someone else.

speaker: Agriculture Policy Director
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. The country imports almost all its cooking oil, draining dollars and leaving prices hostage to world markets. A push for domestic mustard, sunflower, and soy oilseed would build self-reliance — slow, and competing for the same scarce land as food grains.

left.label: Drive domestic oilseed self-reliance.
left.tooltip: A kitchen run on imported oil is a kitchen run from abroad.
left.reveal: You incentivise oilseed cultivation and crushing. It competes for scarce land and the payoff is slow, but the cooking-oil import bill begins to fall and prices steady against world shocks.

right.label: Keep importing cheap cooking oil.
right.tooltip: Imported oil is cheap and the land is needed for rice.
right.reveal: You keep relying on imported edible oil. The land stays in grain and the oil stays cheap on the shelf, and every world price spike passes straight into the nation's kitchens.

speaker: Sugar Corporation Chief
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. The state sugar mills lose money every year on obsolete machinery and graft, but tens of thousands of cane farmers and mill workers depend on them. The chief can shut or privatise the worst, stemming the losses, or pour in another bailout and modernisation gamble.

left.label: Shut or privatise the loss-making mills.
left.tooltip: A mill that only crushes losses crushes nothing.
left.reveal: You close or sell the worst mills and stem the bleeding. The freed budget is real, and the cane farmers and mill towns built around the mills are left stranded and furious.

right.label: Bail out and modernise the mills.
right.tooltip: The cane belt has nowhere else to turn.
right.reveal: You fund a modernisation gamble to save the mills and the cane belt. The cost is heavy and the odds uncertain, but the farmers and workers keep their livelihood and a chance at a viable industry.

speaker: Agriculture Marketing Director
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. A third of the fruit and vegetables rot between farm and market for want of storage, cold chain, and roads — a staggering waste that beggars farmers and inflates prices. The director wants a costly cold-chain and storage build; the capital is large and the gain diffuse.

left.label: Build the cold chain and storage.
left.tooltip: A harvest that rots on the road feeds no one and ruins the grower.
left.reveal: You invest in cold storage, packhouses, and farm-to-market links. The capital is heavy, but the rotting waste falls sharply, farmers earn more, and consumers pay less for what no longer spoils.

right.label: Leave the supply chain as it is.
right.tooltip: A cold chain is a fortune for a problem the market lives with.
right.reveal: You leave the produce to rot as it always has. The capital is spared, and a third of the harvest keeps spoiling between farm and plate, beggaring farmers and inflating prices.

speaker: Industries Resource Officer
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. The coal plants pile up fly-ash as toxic waste, while the brick kilns devour the country's topsoil. Mandating fly-ash bricks would solve both — using the waste, sparing the soil — but it disrupts the entrenched clay-brick industry and its army of workers.

left.label: Mandate fly-ash brick production.
left.tooltip: A waste that builds is better than a soil that burns.
left.reveal: You mandate fly-ash bricks. The clay-kiln industry resists and some workers are displaced, but the toxic ash finds a use, the topsoil is spared, and the smoggy clay kilns lose their grip.

right.label: Leave the clay-brick industry alone.
right.tooltip: A million kiln workers outweigh a pile of ash.
right.reveal: You leave the clay kilns be. The workers keep their jobs, and the fly-ash keeps piling up toxic while the kilns keep burning the country's topsoil into bricks.

speaker: Marine Resources Director
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. Declaring a large marine protected area in the bay would let the overfished stocks recover and safeguard biodiversity — a long-term gift to the fishery — but it locks the trawler fleet and the artisanal fishers out of rich grounds in the short term.

left.label: Declare the marine protected area.
left.tooltip: A sea given rest is a sea that feeds again.
left.reveal: You ring off the protected area and the stocks within it rebound, spilling fish into the surrounding waters over time. The trawlers and fishers locked out protest the short-term loss bitterly.

right.label: Keep the grounds open to fishing.
right.tooltip: A fisher kept off the water today does not eat today.
right.reveal: You leave the rich grounds open. The fishers keep their catch this season, and the overfished stocks slide further toward collapse, taking the fishery's future down with them.

speaker: Grid Expansion Director
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. New power plants sit idle for want of transmission lines to evacuate their power, and the lines are stalled by land disputes and farmers refusing the towers across their fields. The director can force the right-of-way through with compulsory acquisition, or negotiate slow and fair.

left.label: Force the right-of-way through.
left.tooltip: A plant that cannot send its power is a monument.
left.reveal: You push the lines through with compulsory acquisition. The towers rise fast and the stranded plants finally deliver, and the dispossessed farmers, paid little and asked less, nurse a lasting grievance.

right.label: Negotiate fair, slow right-of-way.
right.tooltip: A tower on a man's field should be a deal, not a decree.
right.reveal: You negotiate fair compensation and routing with the farmers. The lines come slowly and the plants stay idle longer, but the right-of-way is won by consent rather than coercion.

speaker: Plantation Affairs Officer
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. Tea companies want to expand their estates into adjoining reserve forest and indigenous land — more export tea, at the cost of felled forest and displaced hill communities. The officer asks whether to grant the leases or protect the forest and the people on it.

left.label: Grant the tea-estate expansion.
left.tooltip: Tea is dollars; forest is just trees.
left.reveal: You grant the leases and the estates spread into the forest. Export tea grows, and felled forest and displaced hill communities pay for it, and another grievance hardens in the hills.

right.label: Protect the forest and the communities.
right.tooltip: An estate built on a felled forest and a displaced people is a poor brew.
right.reveal: You refuse the expansion into forest and indigenous land. The tea companies lose their growth and grumble, but the forest stands and the hill communities keep the ground beneath them.

speaker: Irrigation Technology Director
prompt: Late in the reign, the old question demands a heavier answer. Replacing the diesel and grid pumps that irrigate the fields with solar-powered ones would slash the fuel bill and emissions and free farmers from fuel-price shocks — a large upfront cost, against the cheap, familiar diesel status quo.

left.label: Drive the solar-irrigation rollout.
left.tooltip: A pump that runs on sunlight runs on nothing dear.
left.reveal: You subsidise solar irrigation pumps across the farmland. The upfront cost is large, but farmers escape diesel-price shocks, the fuel bill and emissions fall, and the fields water themselves on sunshine.

right.label: Stick with diesel and grid pumps.
right.tooltip: A solar pump is a fortune against a jerrycan of diesel.
right.reveal: You leave irrigation on diesel and grid power. The upfront cost is dodged, and the farmers stay hostage to fuel prices while the pumps keep burning diesel and draining the subsidy.

speaker: Coastal Fisheries Officer
prompt: It returns once more — and this round may be the one remembered. The dried-fish trade — a coastal staple and growing export — is rife with illegal pesticide use to keep flies off the drying racks, poisoning consumers and threatening the export market. The officer wants enforcement and clean drying tech; the traders resist the cost.

left.label: Enforce clean drying, ban the pesticides.
left.tooltip: A staple cured in poison is a slow harm to millions.
left.reveal: You ban the pesticides and fund clean drying technology. The traders resist the cost, but the dried fish on the nation's plates and in the export crates stops carrying its hidden poison.

right.label: Leave the dried-fish trade alone.
right.tooltip: The trade is cheap and the demand is high.
right.reveal: You leave the drying racks and their pesticides be. The trade stays cheap and busy, and the dried fish keeps reaching plates and export markets laced with the chemicals that keep the flies off.

speaker: Geological Survey Director
prompt: The reckoning circles back, grown teeth in the waiting. A thorough national mineral survey could reveal coal, gas, hard rock, and rare minerals worth fortunes — knowledge that is power, and a magnet for foreign extractors and the conflicts that follow discovery. The director asks whether to survey aggressively or tread lightly.

left.label: Survey aggressively and map the wealth.
left.tooltip: A nation ignorant of its own ground is poorer for it.
left.reveal: You fund an aggressive survey and the map of the country's mineral wealth fills in. Knowledge is gained, and so is the attention of foreign extractors and the disputes that ride on every discovery.

right.label: Survey cautiously and slowly.
right.tooltip: A discovery is a curse as often as a blessing.
right.reveal: You proceed with a slow, cautious survey. You forgo the rush of knowledge and investment, and avoid lighting the fuse of extraction conflicts and foreign appetite that a full mineral map would.

speaker: Coastal Afforestation Officer
prompt: Another term, the same antagonist, the stakes raised again. Planting forests on the new coastal chars would stabilise the silt islands, buffer storms, and eventually create land for the landless — slow, unglamorous work, easily neglected, competing with the rush to hand the chars to shrimp farms and grabbers.

left.label: Fund char afforestation and stabilisation.
left.tooltip: A planted char becomes solid ground for the landless.
left.reveal: You fund the slow work of foresting and stabilising the chars. The shrimp interests and grabbers eye the land hungrily, but the islands solidify, buffer the storms, and become future homes for the landless.

right.label: Hand the chars to shrimp and developers.
right.tooltip: The chars earn more in ponds than in saplings.
right.reveal: You let the chars go to shrimp farms and grabbers. The export earnings flow and the powerful are pleased, and the unstabilised islands stay storm-prone and the landless landless.

speaker: Land Reform Commissioner
prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. Land-ceiling laws meant to cap how much one family can own are flouted wholesale through benami holdings, while millions are landless. The commissioner wants real enforcement and redistribution of the surplus; the great landholders, many connected, will fight to the death.

left.label: Enforce the land ceiling and redistribute.
left.tooltip: Land hoarded past the law is land owed to the landless.
left.reveal: You enforce the ceiling, pierce the benami holdings, and redistribute surplus land. The great landholders rage and pull every string, but millions of the landless gain ground of their own.

right.label: Leave the land ceiling unenforced.
right.tooltip: The great landholders are the spine of rural power.
right.reveal: You leave the ceiling a dead letter. The connected landlords keep their vast holdings behind false names, and the landless millions stay landless, tenants and labourers on ground the law says they should share.

speaker: Farm Mechanisation Director
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. Mechanising the farms — harvesters, tillers, transplanters — would raise yields and ease the brutal labour, especially as workers leave for the cities. But it would also throw landless farm labourers, who have nothing else, out of their only work. The director asks how hard to push.

left.label: Push mechanisation hard.
left.tooltip: A farm that cannot find hands must find machines.
left.reveal: You subsidise farm machinery widely. Yields rise and the labour eases as workers drift cityward, and the landless labourers who relied on harvest wages find the machines have taken their last work.

right.label: Mechanise gently, protect farm labour.
right.tooltip: A machine that idles the landless feeds the slum, not the field.
right.reveal: You phase mechanisation in slowly with retraining for displaced labourers. Yields rise more modestly, but the landless are not thrown off the land all at once into the cities' slums.

speaker: Industrial Waste Officer
prompt: The same fault line, the harder hour, the narrower path. Used engine oil and scrap tyres are dumped or burned in toxic backyard operations that poison soil and air. A formal recycling and pyrolysis industry could turn the waste to value cleanly — at a cost the informal burners and dumpers will resist.

left.label: Build formal oil and tyre recycling.
left.tooltip: A tyre burned in a yard is a poison breathed in a slum.
left.reveal: You license clean recycling and pyrolysis and crack down on backyard burning. The informal operators resist losing their cheap trade, but the toxic dumping and burning recede and the waste finds clean value.

right.label: Leave waste-oil and tyres to the informal trade.
right.tooltip: The backyard burners harm no one the budget answers for.
